verses.pali:
Taṇhāya jāyatī soko, taṇhāya jāyatī bhayaṁ;
Taṇhāya vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayaṁ.
verses.translation: Sorrow springs from craving, fear springs from craving; one free from craving has no sorrow, let alone fear.
verses.commentary: This verse addresses 'taṇhā' (craving/thirst), the most fundamental form of attachment in Buddhist teaching. Taṇhā is identified as the second Noble Truth—the cause of all suffering. It includes craving for sensual pleasures (kāma-taṇhā), craving for existence (bhava-taṇhā), and craving for non-existence (vibhava-taṇhā). This deep-seated thirst drives the cycle of rebirth and perpetuates suffering. The complete eradication of craving is equivalent to attaining Nibbāna (Nirvana), the ultimate goal of Buddhist practice. When craving is extinguished, both grief and fear cease entirely, as there is nothing left to be desired, grasped, or feared losing.

story.title: The Story of a Brāhmin
story.content: While residing at the Jetavana Monastery, the Buddha spoke this verse with reference to a Brāhmin who was a farmer. This Brāhmin held wrong views and was very attached to his farmland and crops. One day, he went to the bank of the river to work in his field. He was extremely proud of his extensive land holdings and had great expectations for a bountiful harvest. The Brāhmin had invested all his wealth and energy into his farming, and his entire sense of security and future happiness depended on the success of his crops. However, just when his crops were ready for harvest, a severe storm came with heavy rain and strong winds, completely destroying all his hard work. His fields were flooded, and all his crops were ruined. Overcome with grief at the loss of his anticipated harvest and wealth, and filled with fear about his future, the Brāhmin was in deep distress. Someone advised him to go see the Buddha, suggesting that the Buddha's teaching might bring him solace. Though initially reluctant due to his attachment to wrong views, the Brāhmin eventually went to the Jetavana Monastery. The Buddha, seeing his mental state, knew that he could be receptive to the Dhamma. The Buddha taught him this verse, explaining that his grief over the loss and his fear about the future both arose from his craving—his intense desire for wealth, success, and security through his farming. The Buddha explained that craving is the root cause of all suffering, and that as long as one is bound by craving, one cannot escape grief and fear. Only by freeing oneself from craving can one attain true peace and happiness, which are not dependent on external conditions. Understanding this teaching, the Brāhmin began to see the futility of his attachments and started to develop right view. He realized that his suffering was self-created through his craving and attachment, and this understanding set him on the path toward liberation.

verses.transliteration:
Tanhaya jayati soko, tanhaya jayati bhayam;
Tanhaya vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayam.

word_meanings.term: Taṇhāya jāyatī soko, taṇhāya jāyatī bhayaṁ
word_meanings.meaning: From craving there arises grief, from craving there arises fear,

word_meanings.term: taṇhāya vippamuttassa natthi soko, kuto bhayaṁ?
word_meanings.meaning: for one who is free from craving there is no grief, how is there fear?

verses.share_excerpt: Sorrow springs from craving, fear springs from craving; one free from craving has no sorrow, let alone fear.
verses.seo_description: Sorrow springs from craving, fear springs from craving; one free from craving has no sorrow, let alone fear.

verses.pali:
Sīladassanasampannaṁ, dhammaṭṭhaṁ saccavedinaṁ;
Attano kamma kubbānaṁ, taṁ jano kurute piyaṁ.
verses.translation: One accomplished in virtue and vision, firmly principled, and truthful, doing oneself what ought be done: that’s who the people love.
verses.commentary: This verse presents the positive counterpart to the previous warnings about attachment. It describes the qualities of an ideal person—one who is morally pure (sīla), possesses wisdom and insight (dassana), is firmly established in the Dhamma, has realized the Four Noble Truths, and diligently performs their duties. Such a person naturally earns the respect, love, and esteem of others, not through manipulation or self-promotion, but through the inherent nobility of their character. This teaching shows that true worthiness of love comes not from seeking it, but from developing one's spiritual qualities. People naturally feel drawn to and hold dear those who embody these virtues.

story.title: The Story of Five Hundred Boys
story.content: While residing at the Veluvana Monastery in Rājagaha, the Buddha spoke this verse with reference to five hundred boys. On a festival day, the Buddha entered the city of Rājagaha for alms, accompanied by a number of bhikkhus. On their way, they encountered five hundred boys who were on their way to a pleasure garden, carrying baskets of pancakes for their picnic. When the boys saw the Buddha and his monks, they paid their respects but did not offer anything to them. The Buddha said to his disciples, 'Bhikkhus, you shall eat those pancakes today. The owner is coming close behind us. We shall proceed only after taking some of these pancakes.' The monks were puzzled by this statement, as the boys had already passed by without offering anything. As they continued walking, they came upon the Venerable Mahā Kassapa, who was walking some distance behind them. The moment the boys saw Mahā Kassapa, they were immediately filled with devotion and joy. They quickly approached him with great respect, and without any prompting, offered him all their pancakes, keeping nothing for themselves. Elder Kassapa, knowing the Buddha's prediction, advised the boys to go back and offer the pancakes to the Buddha and the Sangha who were sitting nearby waiting. The boys gladly did so, and the Buddha and his disciples accepted and ate the pancakes. Some monks were puzzled and even slightly annoyed that the boys had shown such favoritism toward Elder Kassapa and not toward the Buddha or the other monks. They mentioned this to the Buddha, questioning why the boys had not been initially inspired to make offerings but only when they saw Kassapa. The Buddha explained that this was due to the special qualities of Elder Kassapa. He told them that Mahā Kassapa was perfect in virtue and insight, firmly established in the Dhamma, had realized all the Truths, and was diligent in fulfilling all his duties. The Buddha said that even the devas (celestial beings) held Kassapa dear because of these qualities. The Buddha then spoke this verse, explaining that a person who possesses such virtues naturally inspires love and devotion in others—not because they seek it, but because the purity and nobility of their character naturally draws people to them. This teaching helped the monks understand that true spiritual attainment manifests in ways that naturally inspire others, and that worthiness of respect comes from inner development, not from external status or position.

verses.transliteration:
Siladassanasampannam, dhammattham saccavedinam;
Attano kamma kubbanam, tam jano kurute piyam.

word_meanings.meaning: Endowed with virtue and insight, principled, knowing the truths,

word_meanings.term: attano kamma’ kubbānaṁ, taṁ jano kurute piyaṁ
word_meanings.meaning: doing the deeds that are his own, that one the people love.

verses.share_excerpt: One accomplished in virtue and vision, firmly principled, and truthful, doing oneself what ought be done: that’s who the people love.
verses.seo_description: One accomplished in virtue and vision, firmly principled, and truthful, doing oneself what ought be done: that’s who the people love.

verses.pali:
Chandajāto anakkhāte, Manasā ca phuṭo siyā;
Kāmesu ca appaṭibaddhacitto, Uddhaṁsototi vuccati.
verses.translation: One eager to realize the ineffable would be filled with awareness. Their mind not bound to pleasures of sense, they’re said to be heading upstream.
verses.commentary: This verse describes someone who has developed a strong aspiration for Nibbāna, the ultimate reality that cannot be adequately described in words (anakkhāta). Their mind is deeply touched by and oriented toward this transcendent goal. Most importantly, their consciousness is no longer entangled in sensual desires. Such a practitioner is called 'uddhaṃsota' (bound upstream) because they are swimming against the current of worldly existence, heading toward liberation rather than being carried downstream by craving and ignorance. This refers specifically to Anāgāmis (Non-returners), those who have reached the third stage of enlightenment and will not return to the sensual realm but will achieve final liberation in the higher heavens.

story.title: The Story of an Anāgāmi Venerable
story.content: While residing at the Jetavana Monastery, the Buddha spoke this verse with reference to a bhikkhu who had attained the fruit of Anāgāmi (Non-returner), the third stage of enlightenment. This venerable monk had been practicing diligently for many years. Through his sustained effort in meditation and mindfulness, he had gradually overcome the five lower fetters that bind beings to the sensual realm: identity view, doubt, attachment to rites and rituals, sensual desire, and ill-will. Having completely eradicated these fetters, he had attained the state of Anāgāmi. Other monks noticed a remarkable change in this bhikkhu. He seemed to radiate a special kind of peace and contentment. His eyes reflected a deep inner joy, and his demeanor showed complete equanimity in all situations. He was no longer affected by sensual attractions or aversions—his mind remained perfectly balanced whether he encountered pleasant or unpleasant experiences. The monks were curious about this transformation and asked the Buddha about it. The Buddha explained that this bhikkhu had developed an intense desire for Nibbāna, the ineffable state beyond all conditioned existence. His mind had been deeply touched by the reality of this supreme peace, and as a result, his consciousness was no longer bound to sensual pleasures. The Buddha explained that such a person is called 'uddhaṃsota' (bound upstream) because, unlike ordinary beings who are carried downstream by the currents of craving and become, this practitioner was swimming against the current, heading toward complete liberation. The Buddha further clarified that this bhikkhu, having become an Anāgāmi, would not be reborn again in the sensual realm. Even if he did not attain full Arahatship in this life, he would be reborn in the Pure Abodes (Suddhāvāsa), celestial realms accessible only to Anāgāmis, where he would definitely attain final Nibbāna without ever returning to the human world. This teaching inspired the other monks to intensify their own practice, understanding that such liberation was indeed possible through diligent effort and proper practice of the Dhamma.

verses.transliteration:
Chandajato anakkhate, Manasa ca phuto siya;
Kamesu ca appatibaddhacitto, Uddhamsototi vuccati.

word_meanings.term: Chandajāto anakkhāte, manasā ca phuṭo siyā
word_meanings.meaning: The one with desire arisen for the undeclared, will be suffused with the (awakening) mind,
with a mind unconnected with sense pleasures, he is spoken of as ‘one gone upstream’.

verses.share_excerpt: One eager to realize the ineffable would be filled with awareness. Their mind not bound to pleasures of sense, they’re said to be heading upstream.
verses.seo_description: One eager to realize the ineffable would be filled with awareness. Their mind not bound to pleasures of sense, they’re said to be heading upstream.

verses.pali:
Cirappavāsiṁ purisaṁ, dūrato sotthimāgataṁ;
Ñātimittā suhajjā ca, abhinandanti āgataṁ.
verses.translation: When a man returns safely after a long time spent abroad, family, friends, and loved ones celebrate his return.
verses.commentary: This verse uses a beautiful simile to illustrate the karmic results of good deeds. Just as family and friends rejoice when a beloved person returns home safely after a long absence abroad, so too will the fruits of one's meritorious actions welcome the doer of good deeds when they pass from this life to the next. This teaching emphasizes that our actions create consequences that follow us beyond death, and that good karma acts as a friend and protector in future existences. The verse creates a warm, relatable image that helps listeners understand the abstract concept of karmic results in concrete, emotional terms.

story.title: The Story of Nandiya (Part 1)
story.content: While residing at the Isipatana wood near Benares, the Buddha spoke verses 219 and 220 with reference to Nandiya, a wealthy man from Benares. Nandiya was an extremely devout and generous supporter of the Buddha and the Sangha. He was known throughout Benares for his charitable nature and his deep faith in the Triple Gem (Buddha, Dhamma, and Sangha). One day, after listening to the Buddha's discourse on the great benefits and merit of building monasteries for the Sangha, Nandiya was deeply inspired. He decided that he would undertake such a project as his greatest offering. Consulting with the Buddha and the senior monks about what would be most beneficial, Nandiya began planning and overseeing the construction of a large and beautiful monastery. He spared no expense in this endeavor, using the finest materials and employing the best craftsmen. The monastery, which came to be known as the Mahāvihāra (Great Monastery), was built on a spacious plot of land at Isipatana. It included living quarters for many monks, a hall for Dhamma talks, meditation spaces, and all necessary facilities for the monastic community. Throughout the construction, Nandiya personally supervised the work, ensuring that everything was done with the utmost care and devotion. He viewed this not merely as a construction project, but as a sacred offering to the Buddha and a gift to future generations of spiritual seekers. After several months of dedicated effort, the monastery was finally completed. It stood magnificent and serene, a testament to Nandiya's faith and generosity. The day arrived for the formal dedication ceremony.

verses.transliteration:
Cirappavasim purisam, durato sotthimagatam;
Natimitta suhajja ca, abhinandanti agatam.

word_meanings.meaning: When one who lives abroad for a long time comes safely from afar,
his relatives, friends and companions come and greatly rejoice.

verses.share_excerpt: When a man returns safely after a long time spent abroad, family, friends, and loved ones celebrate his return.
verses.seo_description: When a man returns safely after a long time spent abroad, family, friends, and loved ones celebrate his return.

verses.pali:
Tatheva katapuññampi, asmā lokā paraṁ gataṁ;
Puññāni paṭigaṇhanti, piyaṁ ñātīva āgataṁ. Piyavaggo soḷasamo.
verses.translation: Just so, when one who has done good goes from this world to the next, their good deeds receive them there, as family welcomes home one they love.
verses.commentary: This verse completes the simile begun in verse 219, drawing the explicit parallel between the joyful homecoming and the karmic reception in the afterlife. Just as loving relatives eagerly await and warmly welcome a returning family member, our accumulated merit (puñña) awaits us in the next existence, ready to provide favorable conditions and happiness. This teaching provides comfort and motivation: our good deeds are not lost or wasted but accompany us beyond death, creating the foundation for future happiness and progress on the spiritual path. The verse emphasizes the continuity of karma across lifetimes and the importance of performing good deeds as preparation for future existences.

story.title: The Story of Nandiya (Part 2)
story.content: On the day of the dedication ceremony for the Mahāvihāra monastery, Nandiya invited the Buddha and a large gathering of monks to receive the offering. After the Buddha and the Sangha had taken their meal, Nandiya performed the traditional water-pouring ceremony (dakkhiṇodaka) to formally dedicate the monastery to the Sangha for perpetual use. At the very moment when Nandiya poured the libation water as an act of dedication, something extraordinary happened in the celestial realms. In Tāvatiṃsa, the heaven of the Thirty-Three Gods, a magnificent palace suddenly arose, surrounded by beautiful celestial nymphs and filled with all manner of divine pleasures. The palace appeared spontaneously, as if by magic, gleaming with jewels and adorned with every conceivable luxury. The Venerable Moggallāna, who possessed supernormal powers including the ability to visit celestial realms, happened to be visiting Tāvatiṃsa at that time. Seeing this newly appeared palace, he was curious about its origin. Using his divine eye, he investigated to discover for whom this celestial mansion had arisen. The deities of Tāvatiṃsa told him, 'Venerable Sir, this palace has arisen for Nandiya, the generous donor from Benares who has just dedicated the Mahāvihāra monastery to the Sangha. This palace will be his dwelling place when he passes away from the human world and is reborn here among us. His meritorious deed of building and offering the monastery has created this magnificent result.' When Venerable Moggallāna returned to the human realm, he reported this wonderful occurrence to the Buddha and the assembled monks. The Buddha confirmed that this was indeed true, and then spoke these two verses (219-220) to illustrate the principle of karmic results. The Buddha explained that just as relatives, friends, and well-wishers joyfully welcome a loved one who returns safely home after a long journey abroad, so too do one's good deeds welcome the doer of merit when they pass from this life to the next existence. The meritorious deeds performed in this life create favorable conditions and happiness in future lives, just as Nandiya's generous act of building the monastery had created a celestial palace awaiting him in Tāvatiṃsa. The Buddha emphasized that our good deeds accompany us like faithful friends, providing protection and creating favorable circumstances in future existences. This teaching greatly inspired both lay supporters and monks, showing them the concrete results of generosity and virtue, and encouraging them to continue accumulating merit through good deeds.

verses.transliteration:
Tatheva katapunnampi, asma loka param gatam;
Punnani patiganhanti, piyam nativa agatam. Piyavaggo solasamo.

word_meanings.term: Tatheva katapuññam-pi asmā lokā paraṁ gataṁ
word_meanings.meaning: Just so, when one who has performed merit goes from this world unto the next,
his merits are received just as relatives come to their loved one.

verses.share_excerpt: Just so, when one who has done good goes from this world to the next, their good deeds receive them there, as family welcomes home one they love.
verses.seo_description: Just so, when one who has done good goes from this world to the next, their good deeds receive them there, as family welcomes home one they love.

chapters.short_summary: The Piya Vagga, or Affection, explores the spiritual dangers of attachment and the profound peace that comes from transcending worldly bonds.

chapters.summary: One should abandon anger and pride, overcoming all worldly fetters, for suffering never befalls those who cling to nothing and possess no self. This section teaches that the one who restrains rising anger like a skilled charioteer restrains a swaying chariot is a true driver, while others merely hold the reins. Conflict is conquered through its opposite: anger is overcome by non-anger, evil by good, stinginess by giving, and falsehood by truth. By remaining constantly vigilant and training themselves day and night, the disciplined focus their minds on Nibbana, eventually reaching a state where the fires of resentment are extinguished. Beware of the anger of the tongue, the body, and the mind; the wise exercise self-restraint in every action, ensuring they harm no living being through their speech or conduct.

verses.pali:
Kodhaṁ jahe vippajaheyya mānaṁ, Saṁyojanaṁ sabbamatikkameyya;
Taṁ nāmarūpasmimasajjamānaṁ, Akiñcanaṁ nānupatanti dukkhā.
verses.translation: Give up anger, get rid of conceit, and escape every fetter. Sufferings don’t befall one who has nothing, not clinging to name and form.
verses.commentary: To reach liberation, one must discard 'Kodhaṃ' (anger) and 'Mānaṃ' (pride). 'Asajjamānaṃ' means not being attached to 'Nāmarūpa' (the mental and physical aggregates). An 'Akiñcana' is one who owns nothing because they have let go of the idea of 'mine.'

story.title: The Story of Princess Rohiṇī
story.content: Princess Rohiṇī, the sister of Venerable Anuruddha, suffered from a debilitating skin disease that made her skin scaly and unsightly. Shamed by her appearance, she refused to see the Buddha when he visited her family. Her brother, Anuruddha, advised her that her ailment was the result of past kamma rooted in 'anger' (kodhaṃ). In a previous life, as a queen, she had out of jealousy thrown caustic powder on a dancer, causing the dancer's skin to break out in sores. Anuruddha told her that to heal, she must 'renounce her pride' (vippajaheyya mānaṃ) and perform acts of merit. Rohiṇī sold her jewelry to build a refectory for the monks, personally cleaning and preparing the hall. As her 'anger' faded through service, her skin began to clear. When the Buddha finally met her, he spoke this verse. He taught her that as long as we 'cling to mind and body' (nāmarūpasmiṃ asajjamānaṃ), we are vulnerable to the 'suffering' (dukkhā) caused by our own past actions. He urged her to 'overcome all fetters'—the mental chains of ego that keep the cycle of revenge and illness going. Rohiṇī attained the first stage of enlightenment, and her physical beauty was restored. This story illustrates that physical disease is often a reflection of a 'burning' mind, and only by 'giving up' the internal fire of malice can one find true health and peace.

verses.transliteration:
Kodham jahe vippajaheyya manam, Samyojanam sabbamatikkameyya;
Tam namarupasmimasajjamanam, Akincanam nanupatanti dukkha.

word_meanings.meaning: One should abandon anger, one should abandon conceit,
one should overcome every fetter,
without clinging to mind and bodily form,
sufferings never do befall the one having no possessions.

verses.share_excerpt: Give up anger, get rid of conceit, and escape every fetter. Sufferings don’t befall one who has nothing, not clinging to name and form.
verses.seo_description: Give up anger, get rid of conceit, and escape every fetter. Sufferings don’t befall one who has nothing, not clinging to name and form.

verses.pali:
Yo ve uppatitaṁ kodhaṁ, rathaṁ bhantaṁva vāraye;
Tamahaṁ sārathiṁ brūmi, rasmiggāho itaro jano.
verses.translation: When anger surges like a lurching chariot, keep it in check. That’s what I call a charioteer; others just hold the reins.
verses.commentary: Checking 'uppatitaṃ kodhaṃ' (arisen anger) is a feat of mental strength. The Buddha contrasts the 'Sārathiṃ' (the true master-driver) with the 'Rasmiggāho' (one who merely holds the reins), implying that most people are just passengers to their emotions.

story.title: The Story of the Tree Spirit
story.content: A monk, while clearing land for a monastery, accidentally cut the branch of a tree that was the home of a Devata (tree spirit), injuring the spirit's infant child. Overcome by 'rising anger' (uppatitaṃ kodhaṃ), the spirit raised her hand to kill the monk instantly. However, she suddenly 'checked' her impulse, reflecting that killing a disciple of the Buddha would lead her to a hellish rebirth and encourage other spirits to be violent. She acted like a 'true charioteer' (sārathiṃ) who stops a 'rolling chariot' (rathaṃ bhantaṃ) at the edge of a precipice. Instead of striking, she went to the Buddha and reported the incident. The Buddha praised her, noting that anyone can 'hold the reins' (rasmiggāho) when things are calm, but the true test is when the 'horses' of emotion are charging out of control. He told the monks that the Devata’s restraint was a greater spiritual victory than any external ritual. The Buddha then taught the spirit the Dhamma, and she attained the first stage of holiness. This story teaches that the gap between a destructive impulse and a wise action is where 'true mastery' resides. By refusing to let the 'chariot' of anger carry her into disaster, the spirit saved herself and found a higher path.

verses.transliteration:
Yo ve uppatitam kodham, ratham bhantamva varaye;
Tamaham sarathim brumi, rasmiggaho itaro jano.

word_meanings.term: Yo ve uppatitaṁ kodhaṁ rathaṁ bhantaṁ va dhāraye
word_meanings.meaning: Whoever should hold back arisen anger just like a swerving chariot,

word_meanings.term: tam-ahaṁ sārathiṁ brūmi rasmiggāho itaro jano
word_meanings.meaning: that one I say is a charioteer, other people are just rein-holders.

verses.share_excerpt: When anger surges like a lurching chariot, keep it in check. That’s what I call a charioteer; others just hold the reins.

verses.pali:
Akkodhena jine kodhaṁ, asādhuṁ sādhunā jine;
Jine kadariyaṁ dānena, saccenālikavādinaṁ.
verses.translation: Defeat anger with kindness, villainy with virtue, stinginess with giving, and lies with truth.
verses.commentary: This is the 'Law of Opposites.' The Buddha teaches that defilements are 'conquered' (jine) only by their opposites. Hatred is never ended by hatred, but by 'Akkodhena' (loving-kindness/non-anger).

story.title: The Story of the Slave-Girl Uttarā
story.content: Uttarā was a devout lay disciple who wished to spend two weeks in intensive meditation. She hired a courtesan named Sirimā to look after her husband during that time. However, Sirimā became jealous of Uttarā's merit and, in a fit of rage, attempted to pour boiling oil over Uttarā's head. Uttarā, seeing her approach, did not feel anger; instead, she suffused Sirimā with intense 'loving-kindness' (metta), reflecting that Sirimā was her benefactor who had allowed her to practice. Because of the power of her 'non-anger' (akkodhena), the boiling oil felt like cool water on her skin and did not harm her. Sirimā, seeing the miracle, fell at Uttarā's feet and begged for forgiveness. Uttarā then brought her to the Buddha, who spoke this verse. He explained that Uttarā had 'conquered the angry by non-anger.' He taught that the world is a mirror: if you meet 'wickedness' with 'goodness' (sādhunā), and a 'miser' with 'generosity' (dānena), you dissolve the very foundation of their negativity. Sirimā was so moved by this 'conquest' of her own hate that she became a dedicated follower of the Buddha. This story illustrates that 'metta' is a protective force and a strategic weapon. By refusing to meet fire with fire, Uttarā transformed an enemy into a friend and proved that the 'Truth' (saccena) of non-violence is the only way to end the cycle of conflict.

verses.transliteration:
Akkodhena jine kodham, asadhum sadhuna jine;
Jine kadariyam danena, saccenalikavadinam.

word_meanings.term: Akkodhena jine kodhaṁ, asādhuṁ sādhunā jine
word_meanings.meaning: Through kindness one should overcome anger, through goodness one should overcome a lack of goodness,
through gifts one should overcome stinginess, through truth (one should overcome) lying speech.

verses.share_excerpt: Defeat anger with kindness, villainy with virtue, stinginess with giving, and lies with truth.
verses.seo_description: Defeat anger with kindness, villainy with virtue, stinginess with giving, and lies with truth.

verses.pali:
Saccaṁ bhaṇe na kujjheyya, dajjā appampi yācito;
Etehi tīhi ṭhānehi, gacche devāna santike.
verses.translation: Speak the truth, do not be angry, and give when asked, if only a little. By these three means, you may enter the presence of the gods.
verses.commentary: Three 'stations' (ṭhānehi) lead to the celestial realms: 'Saccaṃ' (Truthfulness), 'Akkodha' (Non-anger), and 'Dāna' (Generosity). Even a small gift ('appasmiṃ') given with a pure heart has cosmic consequences.

story.title: The Question of the Devas
story.content: A group of monks were discussing what simple actions lead to rebirth in the 'presence of the gods' (devāna santike). The Buddha overheard them and explained that one does not need elaborate rituals or immense wealth to reach the heavens. He told the story of a very poor man who had nothing but a single meal for the day. When a traveling monk 'asked' (yācito) for food, the man 'gave' even that 'little' (appasmiṃ) with a heart of pure generosity. Throughout his life, this man also committed to 'speak the truth' (saccaṃ bhaṇe) and 'not yield to anger' (na kujjheyya), even when provoked by those who mocked his poverty. The Buddha explained that these 'three virtues' are the ladders to the higher worlds. Truthfulness purifies the speech, non-anger purifies the mind, and generosity purifies the intentions. By maintaining these three consistently, the man was reborn as a glorious Deva. The Buddha taught the monks that the 'divine life' is built on the foundation of these three qualities. It is not the quantity of what is given, but the quality of the 'non-anger' and 'truth' that accompanies the gift. This story encourages everyone, regardless of their status, to realize that the 'celestial path' is paved with the simple, daily choices of how we speak, how we react, and how we share.

verses.transliteration:
Saccam bhane na kujjheyya, dajja appampi yacito;
Etehi tihi thanehi, gacche devana santike.

word_meanings.term: Saccaṁ bhaṇe, na kujjheyya, dajjāppasmim-pi yācito
word_meanings.meaning: One should speak out the truth, one should not get angry, when requested give, if only a little,

word_meanings.term: etehi tīhi ṭhānehi gacche devāna’ santike
word_meanings.meaning: through these three conditions one can go to the presence of the gods.

verses.share_excerpt: Speak the truth, do not be angry, and give when asked, if only a little. By these three means, you may enter the presence of the gods.
verses.seo_description: Speak the truth, do not be angry, and give when asked, if only a little. By these three means, you may enter the presence of the gods.

verses.pali:
Ahiṁsakā ye munayo, niccaṁ kāyena saṁvutā;
Te yanti accutaṁ ṭhānaṁ, yattha gantvā na socare.
verses.translation: Those harmless sages, always restrained in body, go to the state that does not pass, where there is no sorrow.
verses.commentary: 'Ahiṃsakā' (harmless ones) refers to those who practice universal compassion. Being 'kāyena saṃvutā' (restrained in body) means avoiding all acts of physical violence. The 'accutaṃ ṭhānaṃ' (deathless/changeless state) is Nibbana, where 'grief' (socare) cannot enter.

story.title: The Story of the Butcher's Daughter
story.content: A young girl was born into a family of butchers, but from childhood, she possessed the heart of a 'sage' (muni). She refused to participate in the 'harming' (hiṃsā) of any living creature. Even under pressure from her family, she remained 'ever-restrained in body' (niccaṃ kāyena saṃvutā), ensuring that her hands never took a life. Her parents mocked her, thinking she was weak, but the Buddha saw that she was building the foundation for the 'deathless state' (accutaṃ ṭhānaṃ). He taught her that true 'harmlessness' is the highest form of bravery. While others are busy creating 'grief' for themselves and others through violence, the 'Ahiṃsaka' is building a sanctuary of peace. The Buddha explained that Nibbana is the destination for those who have stopped 'striking' the world. The girl eventually became a nun and, through her unshakeable commitment to non-violence, attained the stage where she would 'never grieve' (na socare) again. This story teaches that 'restraint' is not a limitation, but a 'passport' to a state beyond suffering. By refusing to create the causes of sorrow in others, the sage ensures that no sorrow can take root in their own heart. The path to the 'Deathless' is paved with the lives we choose *not* to take.

verses.transliteration:
Ahimsaka ye munayo, niccam kayena samvuta;
Te yanti accutam thanam, yattha gantva na socare.

word_meanings.term: Ahiṁsakā ye munayo, niccaṁ kāyena saṁvutā
word_meanings.meaning: Those sages without violence, constantly restrained in body,

word_meanings.term: te yanti accutaṁ ṭhānaṁ, yattha gantvā na socare
word_meanings.meaning: go to the deathless (Nibbāna), having gone there they do not grieve.

verses.share_excerpt: Those harmless sages, always restrained in body, go to the state that does not pass, where there is no sorrow.
verses.seo_description: Those harmless sages, always restrained in body, go to the state that does not pass, where there is no sorrow.

verses.pali:
Sadā jāgaramānānaṁ, ahorattānusikkhinaṁ;
Nibbānaṁ adhimuttānaṁ, atthaṁ gacchanti āsavā.
verses.translation: Always wakeful, practicing night and day, focused only on extinguishment, their defilements come to an end.
verses.commentary: Total liberation requires 'Jāgaramānānaṃ' (constant wakefulness). The 'Āsavā' (taints/can kers) of lust, existence, and ignorance only 'atthaṃ gacchanti' (go to an end) when one is 'ahorattānusikkhinaṃ' (training day and night) with the singular focus on Nibbana.

story.title: The Story of the Nun Puṇṇā
story.content: Puṇṇā was a slave-girl who spent her 'day and night' (ahorattā) performing hard labor—grinding grain and fetching water. One cold night, while she was 'ever vigilant' (sadā jāgaramānānaṃ) at her work, she saw the monks out for meditation. She wondered: 'I am awake because I am a slave and have no choice, but why are they awake?' She realized that they were 'wholly intent upon Nibbana' (nibbānaṃ adhimuttānaṃ). This inspired her to use her own sleepless nights for 'training' (anusikkhinaṃ). She began to observe her own breath and the sensations of her body while she worked. Even while grinding grain, her mind was focused on the 'extinguishing of the taints' (āsavā). The Buddha, perceiving her sincerity, spoke this verse to her. He taught her that it doesn't matter what your social status is; if you use every moment—day and night—to be 'awake' to the truth, the 'can kers' of the mind will inevitably dry up. Puṇṇā, through her relentless 'vigilance' in the midst of slavery, attained the fruit of the path and was eventually freed and ordained as a nun. This story serves as a powerful reminder that spiritual 'training' is not limited to a meditation hall. It is a '24/7' commitment to wakefulness that can transform even the most mundane labor into a vehicle for the highest liberation.

verses.transliteration:
Sada jagaramananam, ahorattanusikkhinam;
Nibbanam adhimuttanam, attham gacchanti asava.

word_meanings.meaning: For those who are always wakeful, who train both by day and by night,
who are intent on Nibbāna, the pollutants are laid to rest.

verses.share_excerpt: Always wakeful, practicing night and day, focused only on extinguishment, their defilements come to an end.
verses.seo_description: Always wakeful, practicing night and day, focused only on extinguishment, their defilements come to an end.

verses.translation: It’s always been this way, Atula, it’s not just today. They blame you when you’re silent, they blame you when you speak a lot, and even when you speak just right: no-one in the world escapes blame.
verses.commentary: The Buddha addresses 'Atula' (a lay disciple) regarding the inevitability of 'Nindā' (blame/censure). Whether one is 'Tuṇhim' (silent), 'Bahubhāṇinaṃ' (talkative), or 'Mitabhāṇinam' (moderate), 'natthi loke anindito'—there is no one on earth who escapes criticism.

story.title: The Discontent of Atula
story.content: Atula was a lay disciple who went with five hundred companions to listen to the Dhamma. He first went to Venerable Revata, but Revata sat in 'silence' (tuṇhim). Atula felt slighted and 'blamed' him for being aloof. He then went to Venerable Sāriputta, who 'spoke much' (bahubhāṇinaṃ), explaining the Abhidhamma in great detail. Atula felt overwhelmed and 'blamed' him for being too technical. Finally, he went to Venerable Ānanda, who 'spoke in moderation' (mitabhāṇinam), giving a gentle and simple discourse. Still, Atula was not satisfied and 'blamed' him for being too basic. Finally, he complained to the Buddha. The Buddha smiled and spoke this verse, explaining that this human tendency to find fault is 'porāṇam' (ancient). He told Atula that even the Buddhas are blamed by some, and even the most wicked are praised by some. In this 'world' (loke), it is impossible to please everyone. The Buddha taught that a wise person should not be 'shaken' by the blame of the ignorant, nor 'puffed up' by their praise. He urged Atula to stop looking for perfection in others and instead look at his own 'blaming' mind. This story is a timeless lesson in 'social equanimity.' It teaches us that criticism is often a reflection of the critic's own preferences rather than the truth of the one being criticized. By accepting that 'blame' is inevitable, we can stop trying to avoid it and start focusing on our own integrity.

word_meanings.meaning: This is something of old, Atula, this is not something of today:
they blame the one who sits silently, they blame the one who talks a lot,
they blame the one who talks in moderation, there is no one in the world not blamed.

verses.pali:
Na cāhu na ca bhavissati, na cetarahi vijjati;
Ekantaṁ nindito poso, ekantaṁ vā pasaṁsito.
verses.translation: There never was, nor will be, nor is there today, someone who is wholly praised or wholly blamed.
verses.commentary: The Buddha emphasizes the 'middle' nature of reputation. No one is 'Ekantaṃ nindito' (wholly blamed) nor 'Ekantaṃ pasaṃsito' (wholly praised). Everyone's reputation is a mixture of light and shadow, fluctuating according to the views of others.

story.title: The Middle Path of Reputation
story.content: Following the discourse to Atula, the Buddha elaborated on the nature of human opinion. He explained that in the 'past' (nāhu), 'present' (etarahi), and 'future' (bhavissati), you will never find a person who is 'one hundred percent' anything in the eyes of the world. Even the sun is 'blamed' by those who are too hot and 'praised' by those who are cold. Even the rain is 'blamed' by the traveler and 'praised' by the farmer. The Buddha used this to show that 'praise and blame' are like the wind; they come and go without substance. He taught that if even a 'Fully Enlightened One' is sometimes criticized by the foolish, then an ordinary person should not expect to be 'wholly praised.' He urged his disciples to be like a 'solid rock' that is not moved by the 'wind' of opinion. The goal of the path is to become 'unshakeable' (tādī). When we realize that everyone is a mixture of 'blame and praise' in the eyes of others, we stop taking either personally. This story teaches us to value 'internal' standards of virtue over 'external' reputation. If your own conscience is clear, it doesn't matter if the world 'blames' you; and if your conscience is stained, it doesn't matter if the world 'praises' you. The only thing that 'is now' (vijjati) and 'will be' relevant is the quality of your own kamma.

verses.transliteration:
Na cahu na ca bhavissati, na cetarahi vijjati;
Ekantam nindito poso, ekantam va pasamsito.

word_meanings.term: Na cāhu na ca bhavissati, na cetarahi vijjati
word_meanings.meaning: There was not and there will not be, and at present there is not found

word_meanings.term: ekantaṁ nindito poso, ekantaṁ vā pasaṁsito
word_meanings.meaning: a person totally blameworthy, or one totally praiseworthy.

verses.share_excerpt: There never was, nor will be, nor is there today, someone who is wholly praised or wholly blamed.
verses.seo_description: There never was, nor will be, nor is there today, someone who is wholly praised or wholly blamed.

verses.pali:
Yañce viññū pasaṁsanti, anuvicca suve suve;
Acchiddavuttiṁ medhāviṁ, paññāsīlasamāhitaṁ.
verses.translation: If, after watching them day in day out, discerning people praise that sage of impeccable conduct, endowed with ethics and wisdom;
verses.commentary: The Buddha contrasts the praise of fools with the 'pasaṃsanti' (praise) of the 'Viññū' (the wise/intelligent). The wise praise only after 'anuvicca' (investigating) 'suve suve' (day after day). They look for 'Acchiddavuttiṃ' (flawless/unbroken conduct) and the combination of 'Paññā' (wisdom) and 'Sīla' (virtue).

story.title: The Investigation of the Wise
story.content: A certain monk was highly praised by the villagers for his quiet and seemingly holy demeanor. However, the other monks noticed that he was only 'holy' when people were watching. The 'wise' (viññū) monks among them did not 'praise' him immediately; instead, they 'observed him day after day' (anuvicca suve suve). They looked for 'flawless character' (acchiddavuttiṃ)—conduct that is consistent whether in public or private. They found that he lacked true 'wisdom and virtue' (paññāsīlasamāhitaṃ). The Buddha explained that the praise of the 'fools' is like a passing shadow, but the 'praise of the wise' is like a stamp of gold. The wise do not look at appearances; they look at the 'unbroken' nature of one's discipline. He taught that a truly 'medhāviṃ' (wise person) is like a mountain—solid from every angle. If you are 'endowed with knowledge and virtue,' you do not need to seek praise; the 'wise' will eventually recognize your worth through their 'investigation.' This story teaches us to value 'depth' over 'surface.' It encourages the practitioner to focus on making their 'character' flawless 'day after day' for their own sake, knowing that the only 'praise' that matters is the one that comes from those who truly understand the Dhamma. True 'endowment' is an internal quality that stands the test of time and scrutiny.

verses.transliteration:
Yance vinnu pasamsanti, anuvicca suve suve;
Acchiddavuttim medhavim, pannasilasamahitam.

word_meanings.term: Yañ-ce viññū pasaṁsanti, anuvicca suve suve
word_meanings.meaning: The one who, after being examined day by day, is praised by the wise,
faultless in conduct, sagacious, attending to virtue and wisdom,

verses.share_excerpt: If, after watching them day in day out, discerning people praise that sage of impeccable conduct, endowed with ethics and wisdom;
verses.seo_description: If, after watching them day in day out, discerning people praise that sage of impeccable conduct, endowed with ethics and wisdom;

verses.pali:
Nikkhaṁ jambonadasseva, ko taṁ ninditumarahati;
Devāpi naṁ pasaṁsanti, brahmunāpi pasaṁsito.
verses.translation: like a pendant of Black Plum River gold, who is worthy to criticize them? Even the gods praise them, and by the Divinity, too, they’re praised.
verses.commentary: A person of perfect integrity is compared to 'Nikkhaṃ jambonadasseva' (a coin of the purest Jambonada gold). Such a person is 'beyond blame' (ko naṃ ninditum arahati). Their light is so bright that 'Devāpi' (the gods) and even 'Brahmunā' (the highest creator deity) join in their 'pasaṃsanti' (praise).

story.title: The Purity of Jambonada Gold
story.content: Venerable Mahā Kassapa was a monk who lived with such extreme purity and 'Ardent Energy' that he was compared to 'refined gold' (jambonadasseva). Even when he was an old man, he continued his austere practices, living in the forest and wearing robes made of rags. Some younger monks 'blamed' him, saying he was being unnecessarily difficult. However, the Buddha defended him, asking: 'Who would dare to blame a man who is like a pure gold coin?' He explained that Mahā Kassapa’s virtue was 'stainless.' He told the monks that even the 'gods' (devāpi) in the highest heavens look down with 'praise' (pasaṃsanti) at such a being. Even 'Brahma,' the highest of all, recognizes the spiritual 'value' of such a life. The Buddha taught that while the 'fools' might find a reason to 'blame' even gold, the 'wise' and the 'celestials' see only the brilliance of the metal. This story illustrates that a life of 'perfect integrity' creates an 'aura' of respect that transcends the human realm. When you become 'like refined gold' through the purification of your 'anger' and 'lust,' you become an 'object of veneration' for the entire universe. It teaches us to strive for a 'purity' that is so absolute that 'blame' simply cannot stick to it, like water sliding off a lotus leaf.

verses.transliteration:
Nikkham jambonadasseva, ko tam ninditumarahati;
Devapi nam pasamsanti, brahmunapi pasamsito.

word_meanings.meaning: one who is like a golden coin, who is there worthy to blame him?

word_meanings.term: Devā pi naṁ pasaṁsanti, Brahmunā pi pasaṁsito
word_meanings.meaning: That one is praised by the gods, and has been praised by the Brahmās too.

verses.share_excerpt: like a pendant of Black Plum River gold, who is worthy to criticize them? Even the gods praise them, and by the Divinity, too, they’re praised.

verses.pali:
Kāyappakopaṁ rakkheyya, kāyena saṁvuto siyā;
Kāyaduccaritaṁ hitvā, kāyena sucaritaṁ care.
verses.translation: Guard against ill-tempered deeds, be restrained in body. Giving up bad bodily conduct, conduct yourself well in body.
verses.commentary: 'Kāyappakopaṃ' (physical irritability/anger) often leads to 'Kāyaduccaritaṃ' (bodily misconduct) like killing or striking. The Buddha urges one to be 'Kāyena saṃvuto' (restrained in body) and instead 'sucaritaṃ care' (lead a good/pure life) through physical 'Ahiṃsā' (harmlessness).

story.title: The Taming of the Physical Impulse
story.content: A certain monk had a very 'irritable' (pakopaṃ) physical nature; whenever he was annoyed, his hands would immediately move to strike or his face would contort with rage. He was 'unrestrained in body.' The Buddha taught him that 'physical misconduct' (kāyaduccaritaṃ) is the 'smoke' of a 'fire' inside. He urged the monk to 'guard' (rakkheyya) his physical impulses. He taught him that 'restraint' (saṃvuto) is like a fence that keeps a wild animal inside. If you 'give up' the habit of reacting physically, you create the space for 'pure deeds' (sucaritaṃ). The Buddha explained that the 'body' is a tool; it can be used to 'strike' or to 'help.' By choosing to 'lead a pure life in deed,' the monk began to use his 'body' to serve others, fetching water and cleaning the monastery. He found that as he 'restrained' his physical movements, his 'internal' irritability also began to calm down. This story teaches that 'spiritual work' often starts with the 'physical.' We might not be able to stop the 'feeling' of anger immediately, but we can 'guard' our hands and our 'tongue.' By 'leading a pure life' in our actions, we eventually 'train' our mind to follow. The body is the first 'fortress' we must 'secure' on the path to peace.

verses.transliteration:
Kayappakopam rakkheyya, kayena samvuto siya;
Kayaduccaritam hitva, kayena sucaritam care.

word_meanings.meaning: One should guard against bodily anger, one should be restrained bodily,
abandoning wrong bodily conduct, one should have good bodily conduct.

verses.share_excerpt: Guard against ill-tempered deeds, be restrained in body. Giving up bad bodily conduct, conduct yourself well in body.
verses.seo_description: Guard against ill-tempered deeds, be restrained in body. Giving up bad bodily conduct, conduct yourself well in body.

verses.pali:
Vacīpakopaṁ rakkheyya, vācāya saṁvuto siyā;
Vacīduccaritaṁ hitvā, vācāya sucaritaṁ care.
verses.translation: Guard against ill-tempered words, be restrained in speech. Giving up bad verbal conduct, conduct yourself well in speech.
verses.commentary: 'Vacīpakopaṃ' (verbal irritability) manifests as 'Vacīduccaritaṃ' (speech misconduct) like lying, slandering, or harsh words. Being 'Vācāya saṃvuto' (restrained in speech) is the second pillar of 'Sīla' (virtue). One must 'sucaritaṃ care' (speak well/purely).

story.title: The Power of the Restrained Tongue
story.content: A young monk was gifted with a 'sharp tongue' and was often 'verbally irritable' (vacīpakopaṃ). Whenever he felt a slight 'anger,' he would use 'harsh words' and 'slander' (vacīduccaritaṃ) to hurt others. He felt that his 'words' were his 'weapons.' The Buddha called him and taught him that a 'sharp tongue' only cuts the one who speaks. He urged the monk to 'guard' (rakkheyya) his speech as a soldier guards a gate. He taught him that 'verbal misconduct' creates 'thorns' in the mind. By being 'restrained in speech' (vācāya saṃvuto), one allows 'Right Speech' to grow. The Buddha explained that 'pure life in speech' (sucaritaṃ care) means speaking only what is true, kind, and helpful. The monk began to practice 'silence' whenever he felt 'irritable.' He found that by 'holding back' the 'harsh word,' he prevented a thousand 'regrets.' Gradually, his 'speech' became like 'honey'—sweet and healing to those who heard it. This story illustrates that our 'words' have a 'vibration' that affects our own heart first. By 'giving up' the 'misconduct' of the tongue, we 'purify' our own consciousness. 'Restraint' is not about 'not speaking,' but about 'speaking only from the heart' of wisdom.

verses.transliteration:
Vacipakopam rakkheyya, vacaya samvuto siya;
Vaciduccaritam hitva, vacaya sucaritam care.

word_meanings.meaning: One should guard against verbal anger, one should be restrained verbally,
abandoning wrong verbal conduct, one should have good verbal conduct.

verses.share_excerpt: Guard against ill-tempered words, be restrained in speech. Giving up bad verbal conduct, conduct yourself well in speech.
verses.seo_description: Guard against ill-tempered words, be restrained in speech. Giving up bad verbal conduct, conduct yourself well in speech.

verses.pali:
Manopakopaṁ rakkheyya, manasā saṁvuto siyā;
Manoduccaritaṁ hitvā, manasā sucaritaṁ care.
verses.translation: Guard against ill-tempered thoughts, be restrained in mind. Giving up bad mental conduct, conduct yourself well in mind.
verses.commentary: 'Manopakopaṃ' (mental irritability) is the 'root' of the previous two. 'Manoduccaritaṃ' (mental misconduct) includes 'covetousness' and 'ill-will.' To be 'Manasā saṃvuto' (restrained in mind) is the highest 'guard' (rakkheyya). One must 'sucaritaṃ care' (cultivate pure thoughts).

story.title: The Secret Fortress of the Mind
story.content: A monk was very 'restrained' in his body and his speech; he never struck anyone and never spoke a harsh word. However, his mind was 'constantly irritable' (manopakopaṃ). Inside, he was 'slandering' everyone and 'wishing harm' (manoduccaritaṃ) upon those he disliked. He was a 'holy person' on the outside, but a 'war zone' on the inside. The Buddha sensing his struggle, spoke this verse. He taught him that 'mental irritability' is the most dangerous 'taint' because it is 'hidden' (manasā). He urged the monk to 'guard' his thoughts as a king guards his 'secret fortress.' He explained that 'mental misconduct'—thoughts of 'hate' and 'envy'—is the 'poison' that kills the 'spirit.' To 'lead a pure life in mind' (sucaritaṃ care) means to cultivate 'Metta' (loving-kindness) and 'Karuna' (compassion) even for one's enemies. The monk realized that his 'restraint' was only skin-deep. He began to 'watch' his thoughts 'day and night.' Whenever an 'irritable' thought arose, he 'checked' it with 'wisdom.' Gradually, the 'war' inside ended, and his 'mind' became as 'clear' as a mountain lake. This story teaches that the 'ultimate battle' is internal. By 'guarding the mind,' we 'secure' the source of all our 'words' and 'deeds,' proving that 'Mental Restraint' is the final 'victory' over the world.

verses.transliteration:
Manopakopam rakkheyya, manasa samvuto siya;
Manoduccaritam hitva, manasa sucaritam care.

word_meanings.meaning: One should guard against mental anger, one should be restrained mentally,
abandoning wrong mental conduct, one should have good mental conduct.

verses.share_excerpt: Guard against ill-tempered thoughts, be restrained in mind. Giving up bad mental conduct, conduct yourself well in mind.
verses.seo_description: Guard against ill-tempered thoughts, be restrained in mind. Giving up bad mental conduct, conduct yourself well in mind.

verses.pali:
Kāyena saṁvutā dhīrā, atho vācāya saṁvutā;
Manasā saṁvutā dhīrā, te ve suparisaṁvutā. Kodhavaggo sattarasamo.
verses.translation: An attentive one is restrained in body restrained also in speech, in thought, too, they are restrained: they are restrained in every way.
verses.commentary: The Buddha concludes the 'Kodha Vagga' by summarizing the 'Dhīrā' (the wise/steadfast). They are 'Saṃvutā' (restrained) in 'Kāyena' (body), 'Vācāya' (speech), and 'Manasā' (mind). This 'triple restraint' makes them 'Suparisaṃvutā'—perfectly/completely restrained and thus liberated from anger.

story.title: The Perfect Restraint of the Arahat
story.content: A group of monks were discussing what it means to be 'perfect' in the Dhamma. Some said it was 'knowing all the texts,' and others said it was 'having psychic powers.' The Buddha appeared and told them that 'perfection' is 'total restraint.' He spoke this verse to describe the 'Dhīrā'—the wise ones who have achieved the 'triple mastery.' He explained that a person who is 'restrained in body' (kāyena saṃvutā) is safe from violence. A person who is 'restrained in speech' (vācāya saṃvutā) is safe from regret. And a person who is 'restrained in mind' (manasā saṃvutā) is safe from the fires of 'anger' and 'lust.' When all three are 'guarded' together, the person is 'Suparisaṃvutā'—perfectly 'enclosed' in a 'fortress' of peace. The Buddha taught that this 'restraint' is not a 'prison,' but a 'freedom.' It is the freedom from being 'pushed' by one's own impulses. He pointed to an Arahat monk and said, 'Look at him; he is the master of his body, his tongue, and his heart. No wind can move him.' The monks realized that the goal of the path is to become this 'perfectly restrained' being. This story is the 'Grand Finale' of the 'Anger' chapter. It teaches that the 'cure' for anger is the 'complete' integration of 'mindfulness' into every aspect of our existence, leading to a state of unshakeable 'Equanimity' (Upekkha) that is the 'sign' of the 'Wise.'

verses.transliteration:
Kayena samvuta dhira, atho vacaya samvuta;
Manasa samvuta dhira, te ve suparisamvuta. Kodhavaggo sattarasamo.

word_meanings.term: Kāyena saṁvutā dhīrā, atho vācāya saṁvutā
word_meanings.meaning: The wise are restrained bodily, then they are restrained verbally,

word_meanings.term: manasā saṁvutā dhīrā, te ve suparisaṁvutā
word_meanings.meaning: the wise are restrained mentally, they are indeed very well-restrained.

verses.share_excerpt: An attentive one is restrained in body restrained also in speech, in thought, too, they are restrained: they are restrained in every way.
verses.seo_description: An attentive one is restrained in body restrained also in speech, in thought, too, they are restrained: they are restrained in every way.

chapters.short_summary: One should abandon anger and pride, overcoming all worldly fetters, for suffering never befalls those who cling to nothing and possess no self.

chapters.summary: Like a withered leaf, the negligent person stands at the threshold of death with no provisions for the journey ahead. This chapter emphasizes that internal impurities—such as greed, hatred, and ignorance—are far more corrosive than any physical stain. Just as a silversmith removes dross from silver, the wise person gradually and consistently purges the impurities of their own mind. While it is easy to see the faults of others, it is difficult to see one's own; yet, focusing on the flaws of others only increases one's own defilements. The greatest of all stains is ignorance (avijja), and the practitioner is urged to cast off this primary impurity to achieve clarity. Ultimately, there is no track in the sky, and no permanent state in the conditioned world, so the wise focus on the path of purification to reach the Unconditioned.

verses.pali:
Paṇḍupalāsova dānisi, Yamapurisāpi ca te upaṭṭhitā;
Uyyogamukhe ca tiṭṭhasi, Pātheyyampi ca te na vijjati.
verses.translation: Today you’re like a withered leaf, Yama’s men await you. You stand at the departure gates, yet you have no supplies for the road.
verses.commentary: This verse serves as a stark reminder of mortality and the urgency of spiritual practice. 'Paṇḍupalāsa' (withered leaf) represents the aging, deteriorating body. 'Yamapurisā' are the messengers of Yama, the lord of death. 'Uyyogamukha' is the threshold or doorway of departure from life. 'Pātheyyaṁ' refers to provisions - specifically, the wholesome karma (kusala kamma) needed for a good rebirth. The verse warns that one approaches death unprepared, without the merit needed for the journey beyond this life.

story.title: The Story of the Old Brahmin
story.content: An elderly brahmin who had lived a life focused entirely on worldly pursuits - accumulating wealth, enjoying sensual pleasures, and performing empty rituals - came to the Buddha when he was already very old and frail. His body was bent with age, his skin wrinkled and spotted, his hair completely white, and he walked with great difficulty using a staff. He had heard about the Buddha and finally decided to seek spiritual guidance, but only after spending his entire life in worldly concerns. When he arrived at the monastery, the Buddha looked at him with compassion and spoke this verse. The Buddha explained that the old brahmin was now like a withered leaf - his body worn out, his vitality gone, his time nearly finished. The messengers of death were already waiting for him; his remaining time was very short. He stood at the very threshold of death, about to depart this life. Yet he had made no spiritual provisions for his journey - he had accumulated no merit through generosity, developed no wisdom through study and meditation, cultivated no virtue through moral conduct. He was like a traveler setting out on a long and dangerous journey with no food, no water, no map, and no supplies. The old brahmin, deeply moved by this teaching and realizing the truth of his situation, asked the Buddha what he could still do at this late stage. The Buddha taught him to practice whatever good he could in his remaining time - to give generously, speak truthfully, develop loving-kindness, and contemplate the Dhamma. Though his time was short, even a little merit created with sincere motivation would help him. The brahmin devoted his final days to practice and died peacefully.

verses.transliteration:
Pandupalasova danisi, Yamapurisapi ca te upatthita;
Uyyogamukhe ca titthasi, Patheyyampi ca te na vijjati.

word_meanings.meaning: You are now like a withered leaf,
Yama’s men stand waiting for you,
you stand at decay’s door,
with no provisions for the journey found.

verses.share_excerpt: Today you’re like a withered leaf, Yama’s men await you. You stand at the departure gates, yet you have no supplies for the road.
verses.seo_description: Today you’re like a withered leaf, Yama’s men await you. You stand at the departure gates, yet you have no supplies for the road.

verses.pali:
So karohi dīpamattano, Khippaṁ vāyama paṇḍito bhava;
Niddhantamalo anaṅgaṇo, Dibbaṁ ariyabhūmiṁ upehisi.
verses.translation: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll go to the heavenly realm of the noble ones.
verses.commentary: This verse provides the positive instruction following the warning in verse 235. 'Dīpa' (island) means to make oneself a refuge through Dhamma practice - one must rely on oneself and the teachings, not on external saviors. 'Khippaṁ vāyama' urges immediate effort without delay. 'Niddhantamalo' means purged of mental stains (defilements), and 'anaṅgaṇo' means without blemish. 'Dibbaṁ ariyabhūmiṁ' refers to the Pure Abodes (Suddhāvāsa), the heavenly realms where Anāgāmins (Non-returners) are reborn, called 'noble' because only Ariyas attain them.

story.title: Continuation of the Old Brahmin's Story
story.content: After the Buddha spoke verse 235, warning the old brahmin about his unprepared state, he immediately followed with this verse of encouragement and instruction. The Buddha told him not to despair but to act quickly. The word 'island' (dīpa) has deep significance - just as an island provides safety from floods, one must make oneself an island of refuge through Dhamma practice, relying on one's own effort and the Buddha's teachings rather than seeking external salvation. The Buddha urged the brahmin to strive immediately without wasting another moment, to become wise through understanding the Four Noble Truths and the nature of existence. Though his time was short, if he purified himself of mental stains (greed, hatred, and delusion) and removed all blemishes from his conduct, he could still attain a fortunate rebirth. The Buddha specifically mentioned the Pure Abodes - special heavenly realms accessible only to Anāgāmins (Non-returners, the third stage of enlightenment). While the old brahmin might not reach such exalted attainment in his remaining time, the Buddha's teaching showed him the highest possibility and encouraged whatever spiritual development he could achieve. The brahmin took this advice to heart. Despite his advanced age and physical weakness, he practiced with remarkable intensity and sincerity during his final days. He made himself an island through refuge in the Triple Gem, strived urgently in contemplation and meditation, and worked to purify his mind. When he died, he was reborn in a heavenly realm, having made the best use of his remaining time by heeding the Buddha's urgent instruction.

verses.transliteration:
So karohi dipamattano, Khippam vayama pandito bhava;
Niddhantamalo anangano, Dibbam ariyabhumim upehisi.

word_meanings.meaning: One should make an island for oneself,
soon the wise one should endeavour,
removing the stain, blemishless,
you will go to the divine and noble realm.

verses.share_excerpt: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll go to the heavenly realm of the noble ones.
verses.seo_description: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll go to the heavenly realm of the noble ones.

verses.pali:
Upanītavayo ca dānisi, Sampayātosi yamassa santikaṁ;
Vāso te natthi antarā, Pātheyyampi ca te na vijjati.
verses.translation: You’ve journeyed the stages of life, and now you set out to meet Yama. Along the way there’s nowhere to stay, yet you have no supplies for the road.
verses.commentary: This verse continues the urgent message, with slightly different emphasis. 'Upanītavayo' means one's lifespan has been brought close to its end. 'Yamassa santike' - into the presence of Yama, the lord of death, referring to the judgment or accounting that occurs after death. 'Vāso natthi antarā' means there is no inn or resting place on the journey - one cannot pause or delay once death arrives. The verse reinforces that death waits for no one and that the journey into the next life is immediate and unavoidable.

story.title: The Story of the Dying Monk
story.content: A monk who had lived for many years in relative comfort, performing his duties adequately but without great diligence or urgency in meditation practice, suddenly fell gravely ill. The illness came unexpectedly and progressed rapidly. Within days, it became clear he was dying. As he lay on his deathbed, weak and in pain, he was filled with regret and anxiety. He realized he had wasted years in comfortable complacency, always thinking he would practice more seriously 'later' or 'tomorrow.' He had not devoted himself wholeheartedly to purifying his mind or developing deep concentration and wisdom. Now, facing death, he felt unprepared and frightened. His fellow monks gathered around him, and someone quickly summoned the Buddha. When the Buddha arrived, he looked at the dying monk with compassion and spoke this verse. He explained that the monk's life had reached its end - his time was finished. He was now setting out to meet the consequences of his karma, entering the realm of death. There would be no stopping place on this journey, no pause to gather supplies or make preparation. Once death came, the process would be immediate and unstoppable. Yet the monk had made inadequate provisions - his practice had been superficial, his virtue adequate but not strong, his meditation insufficient to provide real refuge. The Buddha's words, though sobering, had a galvanizing effect. In his final hours, the dying monk aroused tremendous energy. Understanding there was no more time for delay, he gathered all his remaining mental strength and practiced with absolute intensity. He focused his mind, reflected deeply on the Buddha's teachings, and let go of all attachment to life. Through this desperate but sincere final effort, he managed to attain significant insight before death, ensuring a favorable rebirth despite his previous complacency.

verses.transliteration:
Upanitavayo ca danisi, Sampayatosi yamassa santikam;
Vaso te natthi antara, Patheyyampi ca te na vijjati.

word_meanings.meaning: You are now advanced in age,
you have come to Yama’s presence,

word_meanings.term: vāso pi ca te natthi antarā
word_meanings.meaning: there is nowhere to dwell in between,
with no provisions for the journey found.

verses.share_excerpt: You’ve journeyed the stages of life, and now you set out to meet Yama. Along the way there’s nowhere to stay, yet you have no supplies for the road.
verses.seo_description: You’ve journeyed the stages of life, and now you set out to meet Yama. Along the way there’s nowhere to stay, yet you have no supplies for the road.

verses.pali:
So karohi dīpamattano, Khippaṁ vāyama paṇḍito bhava;
Niddhantamalo anaṅgaṇo, Na punaṁ jātijaraṁ upehisi.
verses.translation: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll not come again to rebirth and old age.
verses.commentary: This verse is nearly identical to verse 236 but with a crucial difference in the final line. Instead of promising rebirth in the Pure Abodes, it promises freedom from the cycle of birth and aging entirely - this refers to attaining Arahantship, complete liberation. The earlier verse offered intermediate goals appropriate for most practitioners, while this verse presents the ultimate goal. The message remains: make yourself a refuge, practice urgently, purify yourself - but the result here is full liberation from saṁsāra.

story.title: The Story of Various Practitioners
story.content: The Buddha taught these sequential verses (235-238) to different audiences with varying levels of spiritual capacity and readiness. To those just beginning spiritual life or those whose practice had been lukewarm, he taught verses 235 and 236, warning them of death's approach and encouraging them to strive for at least a favorable rebirth in the Pure Abodes or other fortunate destinies. These teachings motivated countless people to begin serious practice and to accumulate merit while time remained. However, to practitioners with greater capacity, stronger determination, and more advanced understanding, the Buddha taught this verse with its promise of complete liberation. He urged them not to settle for mere rebirth, however fortunate, but to aim for the ultimate goal - the complete ending of the cycle of birth and death. Several monks who had been practicing with moderate effort but holding back from total commitment heard this verse and were inspired to practice with absolute dedication. They realized that complete liberation was actually possible, not just a distant theoretical goal. By making themselves true islands of refuge through the Dhamma, striving with maximum urgency, becoming truly wise through penetrating insight, and purifying themselves completely of all mental stains and blemishes, they could actually end the endless cycle of suffering. Several of these monks, energized by this teaching, intensified their practice dramatically. Through sustained effort in meditation and contemplation, they purged themselves of all defilements and attained Arahantship, fulfilling the promise of this verse - they would never again undergo birth, aging, sickness, and death.

verses.transliteration:
So karohi dipamattano, Khippam vayama pandito bhava;
Niddhantamalo anangano, Na punam jatijaram upehisi.

word_meanings.meaning: One should make an island for oneself,
soon the wise one should endeavour,
removing the stain, blemishless,
you will not come to birth and old age again.

verses.share_excerpt: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll not come again to rebirth and old age.
verses.seo_description: Make an island of yourself! Swiftly strive, learn to be wise! Purged of stains, flawless, you’ll not come again to rebirth and old age.

verses.pali:
Anupubbena medhāvī, thokaṁ thokaṁ khaṇe khaṇe;
Kammāro rajatasseva, niddhame malamattano.
verses.translation: A smart person would purge their own stains gradually, bit by bit, moment by moment, like a smith smelting silver.
verses.commentary: This verse teaches the gradual nature of purification. 'Anupubbena' means progressively or in sequence, 'thokaṁ thokaṁ' means little by little, 'khaṇe khaṇe' means moment by moment. The simile of a silversmith is instructive: when refining silver, a smith heats the ore and removes impurities gradually - not all at once, but through repeated cycles of heating and skimming. Similarly, spiritual purification is a gradual process requiring patience, persistence, and repeated effort. One cannot eliminate all defilements instantly but must work steadily, removing mental impurities bit by bit.

story.title: The Story of the Hall Builder
story.content: A devout brahmin who lived near Jetavana monastery would wake early each morning and, in the pre-dawn darkness, observe the monks preparing for their alms-round. He noticed they dressed in a grassy area where the hem of their robes would become wet with morning dew. Moved by compassion, he brought a scythe the next day and carefully trimmed all the grass short. The following morning, he observed that with the grass cut, the monks' robes now became muddy from the exposed earth. So he brought sand and spread it over the entire area, creating a clean surface. Then he noticed that even with the sand, on very hot days the monks would perspire heavily while dressing. So he built a simple pavilion to provide shade. Then he saw that during the rainy season, the pavilion was insufficient - the monks still got wet. So gradually, over months, he constructed a proper hall with walls and a good roof where monks could dress comfortably in all weather conditions. When the hall was finally completed, the brahmin organized a great ceremony, inviting the Buddha and the entire Sangha for a feast. During the celebration, he explained to the Buddha his gradual process of improvement - first cutting grass, then spreading sand, then building a pavilion, finally constructing a complete hall. Each step had addressed a problem he observed, leading progressively to the final result. The Buddha was delighted with this account because it perfectly illustrated the principle he wished to teach. He spoke this verse, praising the brahmin's patient, progressive approach. The Buddha explained that just as the brahmin had gradually improved the monks' dressing area step by step, observing problems and addressing them one at a time, so must a wise person gradually remove mental impurities. Just as a silversmith doesn't try to purify silver all at once but removes impurities bit by bit through repeated heating and skimming, so must practitioners patiently work on their defilements, removing them little by little, moment by moment, until complete purification is achieved.

verses.transliteration:
Anupubbena medhavi, thokam thokam khane khane;
Kammaro rajatasseva, niddhame malamattano.

word_meanings.meaning: The sage gradually, little by little, moment by moment,
should remove the stain from himself, like a smith (removes the stain) from silver.

verses.share_excerpt: A smart person would purge their own stains gradually, bit by bit, moment by moment, like a smith smelting silver.
verses.seo_description: A smart person would purge their own stains gradually, bit by bit, moment by moment, like a smith smelting silver.

verses.pali:
Ayasāva malaṁ samuṭṭhitaṁ, Tatuṭṭhāya tameva khādati;
Evaṁ atidhonacārinaṁ, Sāni kammāni nayanti duggatiṁ.
verses.translation: It is the rust born on the iron that eats away the place it arose. And so it is their own deeds that lead the overly-ascetic to a bad place.
verses.commentary: This verse uses the powerful simile of rust to illustrate self-destruction through one's own actions. Rust (mala) originates from iron itself and then corrodes the iron that produced it. Similarly, a person's own evil deeds, arising from their own mind and actions, destroy that very person. 'Atidhonacārī' refers to one who indulges excessively, particularly in regard to the four requisites (robes, food, shelter, medicine). The verse teaches that we create our own suffering through our own misconduct - the destroyer is not external but internal.

story.title: The Story of Venerable Tissa and the Fine Cloth
story.content: Venerable Tissa possessed eight cubits of coarse, rough cloth suitable for making a monk's robe. Being generous, he gave this cloth to his sister who was also a devoted Buddhist laywoman. When she received the cloth, she examined it and thought, 'This rough cloth is not good enough for my venerable brother. He deserves better quality material.' Out of affection and respect, she carefully unpicked the coarse cloth, thread by thread. Then she spun fine, soft yarn and had it woven into beautiful, smooth cloth of much higher quality. When the cloth was ready, Tissa sent a message asking for his cloth back, as he needed to make his robe. His sister happily sent him the fine cloth she had prepared. However, when Tissa saw the fine, beautiful cloth, he was troubled. As a monk committed to simplicity and non-attachment to luxuries, he felt it was inappropriate to wear such fine material. He initially refused the cloth, asking for the original coarse cloth he had given her. His sister explained what she had done and why, but Tissa remained hesitant about accepting such luxurious material. Other monks heard about this situation and questioned whether Tissa was being too rigid or whether accepting the fine cloth would constitute an attachment to luxury. They brought the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha considered the situation carefully. He knew that Tissa's hesitation came from pure motives - a desire to maintain simplicity and avoid luxury. However, he also saw deeper lessons to teach. The Buddha spoke this verse, using it to teach about how excessive attachment to even the requisites of monastic life, or excessive fussiness about their quality, could become a form of self-destruction. Just as rust arises from iron and then eats away that very iron, excessive preoccupation with the material quality of one's requisites - whether insisting on the finest or refusing anything but the coarsest - could become a subtle form of attachment that would corrode one's practice. The Buddha explained that what matters is the mind's attitude, not the material's quality. If Tissa accepted the cloth with gratitude, without attachment, and used it simply as a practical necessity, there would be no problem. But if he became obsessed with having only coarse cloth, that too could become an inverted form of luxury.

verses.transliteration:
Ayasava malam samutthitam, Tatutthaya tameva khadati;
Evam atidhonacarinam, Sani kammani nayanti duggatim.

word_meanings.meaning: As a (rust) stain arises from iron,
and arisen from that, it eats it away,
so with one who is overindulgent –
his deeds lead him to a bad destiny.

verses.share_excerpt: It is the rust born on the iron that eats away the place it arose. And so it is their own deeds that lead the overly-ascetic to a bad place.
verses.seo_description: It is the rust born on the iron that eats away the place it arose. And so it is their own deeds that lead the overly-ascetic to a bad place.
