verses.pali:
Pāmojjabahulo bhikkhu, pasanno buddhasāsane;
Adhigacche padaṁ santaṁ, saṅkhārūpasamaṁ sukhaṁ.
verses.translation: A monk full of joy trusting in the Buddha’s teaching, would realize the peaceful state, the blissful stilling of conditions.
verses.commentary: Joy (Pāmojja) is not just a byproduct of the path; it is a necessary 'fuel' for it. A joyful mind is easily concentrated, and a concentrated mind sees things as they are. This verse is identical in sentiment to 368, reinforcing the 'happy' nature of liberation.

story.title: The Story of the Joyful Monk
story.content: A certain monk named Pāmojja was always smiling. Even when he was sick, or when he had nothing to eat, or when he was criticized by others, his inner 'joy' (pāmojja) never seemed to fade. Some monks thought he was being frivolous and that the 'holy life' should be more serious and somber. They complained to the Buddha, saying that Pāmojja didn't seem to take the Four Noble Truths seriously because he was always so happy. The Buddha defended the monk, explaining that 'a monk who is full of joy' is actually the one who is closest to the 'state of peace' (padaṃ santaṃ). He explained that sadness and somberness are often just forms of subtle aversion, while joy is a sign of a mind that has found 'faith in the Buddha’s teachings' (pasanno buddhasāsane). He said that joy is the 'moisture' that prevents the heart from becoming dry and brittle. When a monk is joyful, his 'Sankharas' (mental formations) naturally begin to calm down (saṅkhārūpasamaṃ), leading to the highest 'Sukha' (happiness). The Buddha told the monks that Nirvana is not a 'nothingness,' but a 'happy cessation' of all that is stressful and conditioned. Pāmojja’s smile was not because he was ignoring suffering, but because he had found the way out of it. He had discovered that the Dhamma is a path of light, not a path of darkness. Hearing this, the other monks realized that their own 'seriousness' was sometimes just a mask for their inner struggles, and they began to cultivate the 'joy' that comes from a clear and confident heart.

verses.transliteration:
Pamojjabahulo bhikkhu, pasanno buddhasasane;
Adhigacche padam santam, sankharupasamam sukham.

word_meanings.meaning: The monastic, having much happiness, with faith in the dispensation of the Buddha,
could attain to the state of peace, happy in the stilling of (all) conditions.

verses.share_excerpt: A monk full of joy trusting in the Buddha’s teaching, would realize the peaceful state, the blissful stilling of conditions.
verses.seo_description: A monk full of joy trusting in the Buddha’s teaching, would realize the peaceful state, the blissful stilling of conditions.

verses.pali:
Yo have daharo bhikkhu, yuñjati buddhasāsane;
Somaṁ lokaṁ pabhāseti, abbhā muttova candimā. Bhikkhuvaggo pañcavīsatimo.
verses.translation: A young mendicant devoted to the Buddha’s teaching, lights up the world, like the moon freed from clouds.
verses.commentary: 'Daharo' (young) highlights that one is never too young to start. 'Abbhā mutto' (freed from clouds) is a beautiful metaphor for a mind that has cleared away the 'clouds' of defilement to reveal its natural radiance.

story.title: The Story of the Young Samanera
story.content: In the monastery, there was a seven-year-old novice monk (Samanera) named Sumana. Despite his very young age, he was incredibly diligent in his meditation and could sit for hours in perfect stillness. The older monks sometimes looked at him as 'just a child' and wondered if he really understood the depth of the teachings. One day, the Buddha decided to show the assembly the power of the young boy’s practice. He asked Sumana to perform a difficult task that required great spiritual power, which the boy did effortlessly and with perfect humility. The Buddha then spoke this verse to the amazed crowd. He said that a monk, even if he is 'daharo' (young), who truly 'devotes himself' to the path, has the power to 'illuminate this world.' He compared the young monk to the 'moon freed from clouds' (abbhā muttova candimā). Just as the moon is always there but is often hidden by dark clouds, a person’s inner wisdom is often hidden by the 'clouds' of greed and ignorance. By practicing the Dhamma, Sumana had cleared those clouds early in life, allowing his natural radiance to shine through. The Buddha taught that spiritual maturity has nothing to do with biological age; it only has to do with the sincerity of one’s practice. He told the elders that they should not look down on the youth, and he told the youth that they should not wait until they are old to practice. The little Samanera Sumana became a great inspiration for everyone, proving that the light of the Dhamma can shine through anyone who is willing to clear the sky of their own mind.

verses.transliteration:
Yo have daharo bhikkhu, yunjati buddhasasane;
Somam lokam pabhaseti, abbha muttova candima. Bhikkhuvaggo pancavisatimo.

word_meanings.term: Yo have daharo bhikkhu yuñjati Buddhasāsane
word_meanings.meaning: That young monastic who is devoted to the Buddha’s dispensation,

word_meanings.term: sŏ imaṁ lokaṁ pabhāseti, abbhā mutto va candimā
word_meanings.meaning: shines forth in this world, like the moon freed from a cloud.

verses.share_excerpt: A young mendicant devoted to the Buddha’s teaching, lights up the world, like the moon freed from clouds.
verses.seo_description: A young mendicant devoted to the Buddha’s teaching, lights up the world, like the moon freed from clouds.

chapters.short_summary: A monk is defined not by their outward appearance, but by their restraint in eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and speech.

chapters.summary: The final chapter redefines the traditional term Brahmin from a caste designation to a spiritual ideal. A true Holy Man is not one with matted hair or high birth, but one in whom truth and Dhamma abide. Such a person has cut all fetters, is free from anger, and endures reproach and even physical blows without resentment, making patience their greatest strength. They are as clear as the moon, untainted by sensual pleasures like water on a lotus leaf or a mustard seed on the point of a needle. Having laid aside the burden of the self and achieved the Unconditioned, they move through the world without clinging to anything, whether human or divine. By following the path of the trackless ones, the Holy Man reaches the highest goal, becoming a bull among men who has conquered birth and death.

verses.pali:
Chinda sotaṁ parakkamma, kāme panuda brāhmaṇa;
Saṅkhārānaṁ khayaṁ ñatvā, akataññūsi brāhmaṇa.
verses.translation: Cut the stream, striving! Cast aside sensual pleasures, brahmin. Knowing the ending of conditions, know the uncreated, brahmin.
verses.commentary: The 'stream' is the flow of Samsara. 'Akata' means the Uncreated or Unconditioned (Nirvana). A true Brahmin knows the end of 'Sankharas' (conditioned things).

story.title: The Brahmin Who Sought the Stream's End
story.content: A Brahmin named Pasadaka once approached the Buddha, deeply troubled by the relentless nature of his own thoughts and desires. He felt like he was being swept away by a powerful river, unable to find any stable ground. The Buddha, acknowledging the man's struggle, gave him this vigorous instruction. He told him that spiritual life is not a passive activity; it requires one to 'exert yourself' (parakkamma). He compared craving to a 'stream' (sota) that flows from the senses to the heart. To find peace, one must 'cut off' this stream with the sword of effort and 'discard' the heavy weights of sense desires that pull one under. The Buddha explained that the world is made of 'Sankharas'—things that are put together, conditioned, and therefore destined to fall apart. A true Brahmin is someone who has looked deeply into the nature of reality and witnessed the 'destruction of all conditioned things' (saṅkhārānaṃ khayaṃ). By seeing that everything built must eventually crumble, the mind stops trying to build more and instead turns toward the 'Akata'—the Unconditioned or the Uncreated. This 'Akata' is Nirvana, the only state that is not subject to change or decay. The Buddha told the Brahmin that once he truly knows the end of the conditioned, he will no longer be a victim of the stream. He will become a 'knower of the Unconditioned' (akataññū), standing firmly on the farther shore where the water cannot reach. Inspired by this call to action, Pasadaka committed himself to intensive meditation, eventually cutting the stream of his own cravings and attaining the deathless state of Arhatship.

verses.transliteration:
Chinda sotam parakkamma, kame panuda brahmana;
Sankharanam khayam natva, akatannusi brahmana.

word_meanings.term: Chinda sotaṁ parakkamma, kāme panuda, brāhmaṇa
word_meanings.meaning: Strive and cut off the stream, remove desire, brahmin,
knowing the destruction of the conditioned, be one who knows that which is not made, brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Cut the stream, striving! Cast aside sensual pleasures, brahmin. Knowing the ending of conditions, know the uncreated, brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Cut the stream, striving! Cast aside sensual pleasures, brahmin. Knowing the ending of conditions, know the uncreated, brahmin.

verses.pali:
Yadā dvayesu dhammesu, pāragū hoti brāhmaṇo;
Athassa sabbe saṁyogā, atthaṁ gacchanti jānato.
verses.translation: When a brahmin has gone beyond dualistic phenomena, then they consciously make an end of all fetters.
verses.commentary: The 'two states' (dwayesu dhammesu) refer to Samatha (calm) and Vipassana (insight). Together, they allow the monk to reach the 'farther shore' of liberation.

story.title: The Story of the Two States
story.content: A group of learned Brahmins were debating whether the path to God was through deep trance and stillness or through philosophical inquiry and understanding. They brought their debate to the Buddha, who explained that the path is actually a harmony of both. He used the term 'dwayesu dhammesu' to describe the two essential practices: Samatha (tranquility meditation) and Vipassana (insight meditation). He explained that Samatha is like a clear, still lake that allows you to see all the way to the bottom, while Vipassana is the actual seeing and understanding of what lies there. When a practitioner becomes a 'paragu'—one who has gone to the farther shore—in both of these states, they gain a holistic knowledge that is unshakable. The Buddha taught that 'all fetters vanish' (sabbe saṃyogā atthaṃ gacchanti) only when these two powers are combined. If you have calm without insight, you are just sleeping in peace; if you have insight without calm, your knowledge is just dry theory. But when the mind is both perfectly still and perfectly clear, the 'fetters' of greed, hatred, and delusion simply evaporate. He compared this to a man crossing a river with two oars; if he only uses one, he just goes in circles, but with both, he reaches the other side. The Brahmins realized that their debate was based on a false division. Many of them decided to ordain and practice both concentration and insight, eventually finding that the 'vanishing of fetters' was not a mystical myth but a practical reality achieved through the balance of 'two states.' This teaching remains a cornerstone for those seeking a complete spiritual training.

verses.transliteration:
Yada dvayesu dhammesu, paragu hoti brahmano;
Athassa sabbe samyoga, attham gacchanti janato.

word_meanings.term: Yadā dvayesu dhammesu pāragū hoti brāhmaṇo
word_meanings.meaning: When a brahmin has, through two things, crossed over,

word_meanings.term: athassa sabbe saṁyogā atthaṁ gacchanti jānato
word_meanings.meaning: then, for one who knows, all the fetters are laid to rest.

verses.share_excerpt: When a brahmin has gone beyond dualistic phenomena, then they consciously make an end of all fetters.
verses.seo_description: When a brahmin has gone beyond dualistic phenomena, then they consciously make an end of all fetters.

verses.pali:
Yassa pāraṁ apāraṁ vā, pārāpāraṁ na vijjati;
Vītaddaraṁ visaṁyuttaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: One for whom there is no crossing over or crossing back, or crossing over and back; stress-free, detached, that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: This is a highly paradoxical Zen-like verse. Once the ego is gone, the concepts of 'this world' (apāraṃ) and 'Nirvana' (pāraṃ) lose their meaning because there is no 'self' to be in either place.

story.title: The Story of the Shoreless Mind
story.content: Mara, the Tempter, once approached a monk who was deep in meditation and tried to confuse him. Mara asked, 'Where do you stand? Do you stand on this shore of the senses, or do you stand on the farther shore of Nirvana?' The monk, having realized the truth of non-self (Anatta), remained silent. The Buddha, seeing this encounter, explained the profound state of the monk to those standing nearby. He said that for a truly enlightened being, the very concepts of 'this shore' and 'the farther shore' have ceased to exist. 'This shore' represents the internal sense bases (the 'I'), and 'the farther shore' represents the external sense objects (the 'world'). For the Arhat, the boundary between the self and the world has dissolved. He is 'visaṃyuttaṃ'—completely detached from the duality of 'subject' and 'object.' The Buddha explained that as long as you think there is a 'me' that needs to get 'there,' you are still caught in attachment. But when the 'me' is gone, there is nowhere to go and nowhere to stay. Such a person is 'vītaddaraṃ'—free from the 'fever' of fear and anxiety—because there is no longer a 'self' that can be threatened by the world or limited by time. He described this state as being 'shoreless,' like the vast ocean that has no center and no edge. The Buddha told the assembly, 'Him I call a Brahmin.' This teaching was meant to push the practitioners beyond the subtle attachment to the 'idea' of Nirvana itself, encouraging them to find the freedom that exists when all labels and dualities are finally dropped in the silence of total realization.

verses.transliteration:
Yassa param aparam va, paraparam na vijjati;
Vitaddaram visamyuttam, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.term: Yassa pāraṁ apāraṁ vā pārāpāraṁ na vijjati
word_meanings.meaning: For whom the near shore, the far shore or both do not exist,
free of anxiety, being detached, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: One for whom there is no crossing over or crossing back, or crossing over and back; stress-free, detached, that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: One for whom there is no crossing over or crossing back, or crossing over and back; stress-free, detached, that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Jhāyiṁ virajamāsīnaṁ, katakiccamanāsavaṁ;
Uttamatthamanuppattaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Absorbed, rid of hopes, their task completed, without defilements, arrived at the highest goal: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Katakiccaṃ' (whose work is done) is the standard epithet for an Arhat. They have completed the spiritual task and have nothing more to achieve in the cycle of birth.

story.title: The Brahmin and the Arhat
story.content: A Brahmin priest who was very meticulous about his rituals once saw an Arhat monk sitting perfectly still under a tree. The Brahmin was impressed but also skeptical, as the monk didn't seem to be performing any sacrifices or chanting any prayers. He asked the monk, 'What work are you doing? Why are you just sitting there?' The monk smiled but didn't answer. The Buddha, seeing the exchange, explained the status of the monk to the priest. He used the term 'Katakiccaṃ'—one whose work is done. He told the Brahmin that while most people are 'busy' with worldly or religious work because they are driven by 'Asavas' (taints) like craving and ignorance, the Arhat sits in silence because he has already 'attained the highest goal' (uttamatthamanuppattaṃ). There is no more work for him to do because he has already cleaned the house of his mind. He is 'virajamāsīnaṃ'—settled and free from the 'dust' of passion. The Buddha explained that the monk’s meditation is not a task he is performing to get something, but a natural expression of his freedom. He is 'anāsavaṃ'—free from the leaks of desire that cause ordinary people to constantly run here and there. The Brahmin priest realized that his own 'work' of rituals was just a way of trying to reach a goal that the monk had already arrived at. He understood that the highest 'work' is the work of ending all internal 'work' through the realization of the truth. This encounter taught the Brahmin that a true holy person is not defined by their activity, but by the 'done-ness' of their spiritual transformation, sitting in the settled peace of a mind that has no more debts to pay to Samsara.

verses.transliteration:
Jhayim virajamasinam, katakiccamanasavam;
Uttamatthamanuppattam, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: The meditator sitting down, the one who is dustless, who has done his duty, without pollutants,
who has reached the ultimate good, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Absorbed, rid of hopes, their task completed, without defilements, arrived at the highest goal: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Absorbed, rid of hopes, their task completed, without defilements, arrived at the highest goal: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.translation: The sun blazes by day, the moon radiates at night, the aristocrat shines in armor, and the brahmin shines in absorption. But all day and all night, the Buddha blazes with glory.
verses.commentary: This verse compares different types of 'radiance.' While others have conditions for their light (day, night, armor, state of mind), the Buddha's radiance (Tejasā) is constant and unconditional.

story.title: The Radiance of the Buddha
story.content: On a night when the moon was exceptionally full and bright, Ananda remarked to the Buddha how beautiful the world looked under the lunar glow. Earlier that day, they had seen a king's procession where the warriors' armor glittered brilliantly in the sun. Ananda also noted how a great meditating monk they had met seemed to radiate a quiet, golden light. He asked the Buddha about these different types of 'shining.' The Buddha replied with this poetic verse, categorizing the light of the world. He said the sun and moon are kings of the sky, but they are limited by time—one for the day and one for the night. The 'Khattiyo' (warrior) shines with power and authority, but only when he is 'sannaddho'—dressed in his armor and status. The 'Brahmano' (the holy man) shines with the light of 'Jhayi' (meditation), but that radiance is most visible when he is in the depths of his practice. However, the Buddha explained that there is a fourth kind of light: the 'Tejasa' or spiritual glory of a Fully Enlightened One. Because the Buddha has totally uprooted the darkness of ignorance, his light does not depend on the sun, the moon, or a specific meditative state. He 'shines resplendent all day and all night' (sabbamahorattiṃ) because his mind is a permanent source of clarity and compassion. This light is not a physical glare but a spiritual presence that guides and warms all who come near it. Ananda realized that while the world offers many temporary lights, the Dhamma offered by a Buddha is a constant sun that never sets, illuminating the path to freedom for all beings at all times.

word_meanings.meaning: The sun is radiant by day, the moon shines by night,

word_meanings.term: sannaddho khattiyo tapati, jhāyī tapati brāhmaṇo
word_meanings.meaning: the accoutred noble is radiant, the meditating brahmin is radiant,
yet every day and night the Buddha is radiant through his power.

verses.share_excerpt: The sun blazes by day, the moon radiates at night, the aristocrat shines in armor, and the brahmin shines in absorption. But all day and all night, the Buddha blazes with glory.

verses.pali:
Bāhitapāpoti brāhmaṇo, Samacariyā samaṇoti vuccati;
Pabbājayamattano malaṁ, Tasmā “pabbajito”ti vuccati.
verses.translation: A brahmin’s so-called <j>since they’ve banished evil, an ascetic’s so-called <j>since they live a serene life. One who has renounced all stains is said to be a “renunciant”.
verses.commentary: The Buddha redefines these three traditional religious titles based on internal purity: 'Brahmana' (purity), 'Samana' (calmness), and 'Pabbajita' (renunciation).

story.title: The True Meaning of Titles
story.content: A wandering ascetic who was very proud of his status once approached the Buddha and asked how he should be addressed. He believed that his long hair, his special robes, and his lifestyle of wandering made him a 'Pabbajita' (a renounced one) and a 'Samana' (a seeker of peace). The Buddha, however, explained that spiritual titles are not like worldly honors that can be worn like a badge. He said that a person is called a 'Brahmin' not because of his caste, but because he has 'discarded evil' (bāhitapāpoti). The word 'Brahmin' implies a state of being where all unwholesome actions of body, speech, and mind have been pushed away. Similarly, a person is only a 'Samana' if they live in 'samacariya'—a state of constant, peaceful conduct. If one’s mind is full of anger and agitation, wearing a robe does not make them a man of peace. Finally, the Buddha explained that the word 'Pabbajita' literally means 'one who has gone forth.' But 'going forth' is not just about leaving a house; it is about 'giving up one's impurities' (pabbājayattano malaṃ). If you leave your home but take your greed and pride with you, you have not truly 'gone forth.' The Buddha taught that these names are descriptions of a finished internal work. Hearing this, the ascetic realized that he had been focusing on the 'label' rather than the 'content' of his character. He understood that a true holy man is defined by the absence of internal 'dirt' (mala) rather than the presence of external symbols. This teaching inspired him to stop seeking recognition from others and to start the actual work of 'discarding evil' within his own heart.

verses.transliteration:
Bahitapapoti brahmano, Samacariya samanoti vuccati;
Pabbajayamattano malam, Tasma “pabbajito”ti vuccati.

word_meanings.meaning: Warding off wickedness one is called a brahmin,
one living austerely is said to be an ascetic,
* because of driving forth (all) stain from oneself
one is said to be one who has gone forth.

verses.pali:
Na brāhmaṇassa pahareyya, nāssa muñcetha brāhmaṇo;
Dhī brāhmaṇassa hantāraṁ, tato dhī yassa muñcati.
verses.translation: One should never strike a brahmin, nor should a brahmin retaliate. Woe to the one who hurts a brahmin, and woe for the one who retaliates.
verses.commentary: This verse emphasizes non-violence (Ahimsa) and the absolute control of anger. The 'shame' mentioned is a spiritual warning that reacting with hate is a greater failure than being a victim of hate.

story.title: The Story of the Patient Brahmin
story.content: There was a famous Brahmin who had become an Arhat, known for his incredible patience. One day, a man who hated the Buddha's followers decided to test the monk's resolve. As the Brahmin was walking silently for alms, the man approached him from behind and struck him a heavy blow on the back with a stick. The Brahmin did not flinch, did not cry out, and did not even turn around with a look of anger. He simply continued walking as if nothing had happened. When the man saw this, he was terrified by the monk’s lack of reaction and eventually ran to the Buddha to confess his sin. The Buddha used the occasion to teach the assembly about the standard of a true 'Brahmana.' He said that while it is a great evil to 'strike a Brahmin' (na brāhmaṇassa pahareyya), it is an even deeper spiritual tragedy if the Brahmin 'vents his wrath' (muñcetha) in return. He explained that 'shame' (dhī) follows the attacker because of the bad kamma he creates, but 'more shame' (tato dhī) would follow the holy man if he lost his peace. This is because a holy man's primary 'treasure' is his equanimity. If he reacts with anger, he has allowed the attacker to steal his most precious possession. The Buddha taught that the victory of a Brahmin lies in his silence and his refusal to descend to the level of the aggressor. By remaining unmoved, the Brahmin remains superior to the violence. The attacker, moved by the monk's profound dignity, became a devoted student, realizing that true strength is not the ability to hit back, but the ability to remain kind while being hit.

verses.transliteration:
Na brahmanassa pahareyya, nassa muncetha brahmano;
Dhi brahmanassa hantaram, tato dhi yassa muncati.

word_meanings.term: Na brāhmaṇassa pahreyya, nāssa muñcetha brāhmaṇo
word_meanings.meaning: A brahmin should not hit a brahmin, nor should he abandon him,

word_meanings.term: dhī brāhmaṇassa hantāraṁ, tato: dhī yassa muñcati
word_meanings.meaning: woe to the one who strikes a brahmin, further: woe to the one who abandons him.

verses.share_excerpt: One should never strike a brahmin, nor should a brahmin retaliate. Woe to the one who hurts a brahmin, and woe for the one who retaliates.

verses.pali:
Na brāhmaṇassetadakiñci seyyo, Yadā nisedho manaso piyehi;
Yato yato hiṁsamano nivattati, Tato tato sammatimeva dukkhaṁ.
verses.translation: Nothing is better for a brahmin than to hold their mind back from attachment. From wherever a cruel wish recoils, right there suffering subsides.
verses.commentary: This verse links the cessation of suffering (Dukkha) directly to the cessation of 'Himsa' (harm or violence). Restraint from 'piyehi' (dear things) refers to cutting the attachment that leads to defensive anger.

story.title: The Cessation of Suffering
story.content: A Brahmin who was deeply attached to his family and his social standing once complained to the Buddha that he felt a constant 'fever' in his heart. Whenever something he loved was threatened, he felt a surge of violent protective energy that made him miserable. The Buddha explained that for a true holy man, 'there is no better thing' (na brāhmaṇassetadakiñci seyyo) than to practice the restraint of the mind. He said that our 'intent to harm' (hiṃsamano) usually arises when we feel that something 'dear' (piyehi) to us is being taken away. We use violence or anger as a shield for our attachments. However, the Buddha taught that this shield actually burns the one who holds it. He said that 'insofar as the intent to harm is suppressed' (yato yato nivattati), suffering subsides in equal measure. Every time you refuse to react with violence, a portion of your internal 'fever' is extinguished. He compared the mind to a fire; if you stop adding the fuel of 'attachment to the dear' and 'harmful intent,' the fire will naturally go out. The Brahmin realized that his suffering was not caused by external threats to his family, but by his own mental habit of reaching for anger as a solution. He began to practice 'Metta' (loving-kindness) and 'Upekkha' (equanimity), noticing that as his heart became softer and less defensive, his anxiety and grief also disappeared. He learned that the only way to truly protect what is dear is to have a mind that is no longer capable of wishing harm upon anyone, even those who act as enemies.

verses.transliteration:
Na brahmanassetadakinci seyyo, Yada nisedho manaso piyehi;
Yato yato himsamano nivattati, Tato tato sammatimeva dukkham.

word_meanings.meaning: It is no little good for the brahmin:
when the mind is held back from what is dear,
whenever his mind turns back from violence,
then there is a calming of suffering.

verses.share_excerpt: Nothing is better for a brahmin than to hold their mind back from attachment. From wherever a cruel wish recoils, right there suffering subsides.
verses.seo_description: Nothing is better for a brahmin than to hold their mind back from attachment. From wherever a cruel wish recoils, right there suffering subsides.

verses.pali:
Yassa kāyena vācāya, manasā natthi dukkaṭaṁ;
Saṁvutaṁ tīhi ṭhānehi, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Who does nothing wrong by body, speech or mind, restrained in these three respects, that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: This verse emphasizes the 'Three Doors' of action. A true Brahmin's purity is not just a thought; it is a total 'Samvuta' (restraint) across all possible modes of interaction with the world.

story.title: The Story of the Perfect Restraint
story.content: There was a monk who was very quiet and rarely spoke to anyone. Some younger monks thought he was perhaps a bit dull or that he was hiding some secret vice behind his silence. They started to watch him closely, hoping to catch him in a mistake. They watched his 'body' (kāyena) to see if he ever acted out of greed; they listened to his 'speech' (vācāya) to see if he ever told a lie or used a harsh word; and they tried to sense his 'mind' (manasā) during meditation to see if he was distracted. After many months, they had to admit that they couldn't find a single 'dukkataṃ' (evil or wrong action) in him. He was like a polished mirror, reflecting nothing but calm and kindness. They went to the Buddha and asked about this monk’s character. The Buddha spoke this verse, confirming that the monk was indeed a 'Brahmana' in the truest sense. He explained that perfection is found when a person is 'restrained in these three respects' (saṃvutaṃ tīhi ṭhānehi) at all times. Most people can be good in one area but fail in another—perhaps they are physically kind but speak harshly, or they speak kindly but harbor angry thoughts. But the Brahmin is one whose 'Three Doors' are all locked against the entrance of defilements. The Buddha taught that this total alignment is what creates a holy life. It is not enough to just 'think' good thoughts; those thoughts must flow through the body and speech without being corrupted. The younger monks, realizing their own judgmental attitudes were a failure of their own 'mind door,' apologized to the quiet monk and began to focus on guarding their own three respects, inspired by his seamless integrity.

verses.transliteration:
Yassa kayena vacaya, manasa natthi dukkatam;
Samvutam tihi thanehi, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.term: Yassa kāyena vācāya manasā natthi dukkataṁ
word_meanings.meaning: For whom there is no wrong-doing bodily, verbally or mentally,

word_meanings.term: saṁvutaṁ tīhi ṭhānehi, tam-ahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ
word_meanings.meaning: being restrained in (these) three things, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Who does nothing wrong by body, speech or mind, restrained in these three respects, that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Who does nothing wrong by body, speech or mind, restrained in these three respects, that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Yamhā dhammaṁ vijāneyya, sammāsambuddhadesitaṁ;
Sakkaccaṁ taṁ namasseyya, aggihuttaṁva brāhmaṇo.
verses.translation: You should graciously honor the one from whom you learn the Dhamma taught by the awakened Buddha, as a brahmin honors the sacred flame.
verses.commentary: In traditional Brahmanism, the 'Aggihutta' (sacrificial fire) was the most sacred object. The Buddha uses this cultural touchstone to emphasize that the source of Dhamma—the teacher—deserves the highest possible respect.

story.title: Reverence for the Teacher
story.content: A young student once came to the Buddha, asking how he should treat the monks who taught him the Dhamma. He noticed that some students were very casual with their teachers, while others were very formal. The Buddha, knowing the boy came from a family of traditional fire-worshippers, used an analogy he would understand deeply. He said that 'from anyone one should learn the Dhamma' (yamhā dhammaṃ vijāneyya), that person becomes a sacred bridge to the Truth. He told the boy that he should 'revere him' (sakkaccaṃ taṃ namasseyya) with the same intensity and care that a Brahmin priest 'reveres the sacrificial fire' (aggihuttaṃva). In the old tradition, if the fire went out, the ritual was ruined; therefore, the priest watched it with total focus and respect. The Buddha taught that the Dhamma is like a fire that provides light in the darkness of ignorance. The teacher is the one who carries that light and passes it to the student. If the student is disrespectful or inattentive, they 'put out the fire' of learning within themselves. He explained that reverence is not for the 'ego' of the teacher, but for the 'Dhamma' they represent. By showing respect, the student opens their own heart to receive the teaching more deeply. The young boy realized that the teacher was not just a person, but a guardian of the 'Fully Enlightened One's' wisdom. He began to treat his instructors with a new sense of sacredness, finding that his own understanding grew much faster because he was approaching the lessons with a humble and receptive mind.

verses.transliteration:
Yamha dhammam vijaneyya, sammasambuddhadesitam;
Sakkaccam tam namasseyya, aggihuttamva brahmano.

word_meanings.meaning: That one from whom one learned Dhamma taught by the Perfect Sambuddha,

word_meanings.term: sakkaccaṁ taṁ namasseyya, aggihuttaṁ va brāhmaṇo
word_meanings.meaning: with respect bow down to him, like brahmin (bows) at fire-sacrifice.

verses.share_excerpt: You should graciously honor the one from whom you learn the Dhamma taught by the awakened Buddha, as a brahmin honors the sacred flame.
verses.seo_description: You should graciously honor the one from whom you learn the Dhamma taught by the awakened Buddha, as a brahmin honors the sacred flame.

verses.pali:
Na jaṭāhi na gottena, na jaccā hoti brāhmaṇo;
Yamhi saccañca dhammo ca, so sucī so ca brāhmaṇo.
verses.translation: Not by matted hair or family, or birth is one a brahmin. Those who are truthful and principled: they are pure, they are brahmins.
verses.commentary: This is one of the most revolutionary verses in the Dhammapada. It explicitly rejects the 'Jaccā' (birth/caste) system, replacing it with 'Sacca' (truth) and 'Dhamma' (righteousness) as the only valid criteria for spiritual nobility.

story.title: The Story of the Proud Brahmin
story.content: A Brahmin who was very proud of his 'Gottena' (lineage) and his long 'Jaṭāhi' (matted hair) once came to the Buddha and criticized the monks for not following the traditional rules of caste. He believed that he was superior to everyone else simply because of the family he was born into and the outward signs of his asceticism. The Buddha, looking at him with compassion, explained that 'not by matted hair' (na jaṭāhi) does one become holy. He told the man that if a person has dirty thoughts but clean hair, the hair means nothing. If a person is born in a high family but acts with greed, his lineage is worthless. The Buddha taught that a true 'Brahmana' is defined by two internal qualities: 'Sacca' (truth) and 'Dhamma' (righteousness). Truth means seeing things as they really are, and righteousness means acting in accordance with that truth. He said that a person in whom these two things are found is 'suci'—truly pure. External symbols are just 'labels' that people use to deceive themselves and others. The Buddha compared the proud man to a person who wears a king's crown but has no kingdom; the crown only makes him look foolish. The man, struck by the logic and the fairness of the Buddha's words, felt his pride dissolve. He realized that his 'noble birth' was a heavy burden he had been carrying, while the 'noble heart' was something he had neglected to develop. He asked the Buddha to teach him the way to true purity, eventually becoming a monk who was respected for his virtue rather than his ancestors.

verses.transliteration:
Na jatahi na gottena, na jacca hoti brahmano;
Yamhi saccanca dhammo ca, so suci so ca brahmano.

word_meanings.term: Na jaṭāhi na gottena, na jaccā hoti brāhmaṇo
word_meanings.meaning: Not because of matted hair, family or birth is one a true brahmin,

word_meanings.term: yamhi saccañ-ca Dhammo ca, so sucī so va brāhmaṇo
word_meanings.meaning: in whom there is truth and Dhamma, that one is pure, that one is surely a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Not by matted hair or family, or birth is one a brahmin. Those who are truthful and principled: they are pure, they are brahmins.
verses.seo_description: Not by matted hair or family, or birth is one a brahmin. Those who are truthful and principled: they are pure, they are brahmins.

verses.pali:
Kiṁ te jaṭāhi dummedha, kiṁ te ajinasāṭiyā;
Abbhantaraṁ te gahanaṁ, bāhiraṁ parimajjasi.
verses.translation: Why the matted hair, you simpleton, and why the skin of deer? The tangle is inside you, yet you polish up your outsides.
verses.commentary: 'Ajinasāṭiyā' (antelope-skin) was the traditional dress of forest ascetics. 'Abbhantaraṃ' (the interior) refers to the mind, which the Buddha describes as a 'Gahana' (tangled thicket) of defilements if left uncultivated.

story.title: The Story of the Forest Ascetic
story.content: Deep in the forest, there lived an ascetic who spent all his time bathing in sacred rivers, carefully combing his 'matted hair' (jaṭāhi), and ensuring his 'antelope-skin garment' (ajinasāṭiyā) was perfectly clean. He believed that by keeping his exterior spotless and following strict physical rituals, he was becoming a saint. The Buddha visited him and spoke this very direct and challenging verse. He asked, 'What is the use' (Kiṃ te) of all this outward cleaning? He called the man 'dummedha' (witless or foolish) because he was ignoring the most important part of himself. The Buddha said that 'within you is a tangled thicket' (abbhantaraṃ te gahanaṃ)—referring to the knots of jealousy, pride, and desire that were still thriving in the man's mind. He compared the ascetic to a person who washes the outside of a bowl that is full of poison and filth on the inside; no matter how much the outside shines, the bowl is still unusable. The Buddha taught that the 'outside you clean' (bāhiraṃ parimajjasi) is just a performance for others, but the 'inside' is where the actual life is lived. He urged the ascetic to stop focusing on his hair and his clothes and to start 'thinning out' the thicket of his mind through mindfulness. The ascetic was shocked but realized the truth of the analogy. He had been so busy with the 'skin' of his religion that he had forgotten the 'heart' of it. He eventually abandoned his rituals and began the difficult but rewarding work of internal purification, learning that a clean heart is the only thing that truly matters in the eyes of the wise.

verses.transliteration:
Kim te jatahi dummedha, kim te ajinasatiya;
Abbhantaram te gahanam, bahiram parimajjasi.

word_meanings.term: Kiṁ te jaṭāhi dummedha, kiṁ te ajinasāṭiyā?
word_meanings.meaning: Why do you have your hair matted, stupid one, and why your deer-skin?
Within you there is a jungle, you (only) polish the outside.

verses.share_excerpt: Why the matted hair, you simpleton, and why the skin of deer? The tangle is inside you, yet you polish up your outsides.
verses.seo_description: Why the matted hair, you simpleton, and why the skin of deer? The tangle is inside you, yet you polish up your outsides.

verses.pali:
Paṁsukūladharaṁ jantuṁ, kisaṁ dhamanisanthataṁ;
Ekaṁ vanasmiṁ jhāyantaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: A personage who wears robes of rags, lean, their limbs showing veins, meditating alone in the forest, that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: This verse praises the 'Dhutanga' monks—those who live a life of extreme simplicity and solitary meditation. 'Paṃsukūladharaṃ' refers to robes made from discarded rags found in dust heaps.

story.title: The Story of the Lean Forest Monk
story.content: There was a monk named Mahakassapa who was known for his extreme asceticism. He wore 'dusty robes' (Paṃsukūladharaṃ) made from rags he found at cemeteries, and he ate very little, which made his body 'lean' (kisaṃ) and caused his 'veins to stand out' (dhamanisanthataṃ). He preferred to 'meditate alone in the forest' (ekaṃ vanaṃ jhāyantaṃ) rather than live in the comfortable city monasteries. Some of the newer monks, who were used to a more moderate life, wondered if Mahakassapa was taking it too far or if he was just being stubborn. The Buddha, however, spoke this verse to honor Mahakassapa and all those who choose the 'solitary path.' He explained that such a person is a true 'Brahmana' because their physical appearance is a reflection of their total lack of interest in worldly comforts. The 'lean' body is not a result of forced starvation, but a result of being so absorbed in the 'joy of meditation' that physical hunger is forgotten. The 'dusty robes' are a sign that the monk has completely 'vomited' the desire for fashion and status. The Buddha taught that a person who can be happy and settled while 'meditating alone' has found a source of strength that the world can never give and can never take away. He used Mahakassapa as a model for those who wanted to reach the highest stages of realization quickly. The other monks realized that while they were worrying about the quality of their food and robes, Mahakassapa was 'shining' with the light of a mind that had gone beyond all physical needs. They learned to respect the different paths monks take, realizing that for some, the 'forest' is the only home they need.

verses.transliteration:
Pamsukuladharam jantum, kisam dhamanisanthatam;
Ekam vanasmim jhayantam, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: That one who wears discarded clothes, who is lean with protruding veins,

word_meanings.term: ekaṁ vanasmiṁ jhāyantaṁ, tam-ahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ
word_meanings.meaning: who meditates alone in the forest, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: A personage who wears robes of rags, lean, their limbs showing veins, meditating alone in the forest, that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: A personage who wears robes of rags, lean, their limbs showing veins, meditating alone in the forest, that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.translation: I don’t call someone a brahmin after the mother’s womb they’re born from. If they still have attachments, they’re just someone who says “worthy”. Having nothing, taking nothing: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Bhovādī' was a slightly derisive term used by the Buddha for those who relied on their social status and addressed others condescendingly. 'Akiñcanaṃ' means 'having nothing,' referring to the absence of mental baggage.

story.title: The Bhovadi and the True Brahmin
story.content: A young Brahmin man once approached the Buddha and expected to be treated with special deference because he was 'yonijaṃ mattisambhavaṃ'—born of a pure Brahmin mother. He addressed the Buddha with the casual and somewhat superior title of 'Bho' (friend/sir), which was common among the arrogant social elite. The Buddha, however, did not acknowledge his status. He explained that 'I do not call him a Brahmin' just because of his birth certificate. He said that such a person is just a 'Bhovadi'—someone who talks a lot about their importance but has no real spiritual depth. The Buddha taught that as long as a person is 'sakiñcano'—meaning they are full of 'possessions' (not just physical things, but also pride and attachment to their identity)—they are not holy. A true 'Brahmana' is 'akiñcanaṃ'—he has nothing. This doesn't mean he is just poor; it means he has no mental baggage, no 'mine,' and no 'this is who I am.' He is also 'anādānaṃ'—free from the habit of 'taking' or 'grasping' at the world for happiness. The Buddha explained that birth is an accident, but 'attachment-free' living is a conscious choice. The young man, who had always been told he was special because of his bloodline, was initially offended but then realized the profound freedom in the Buddha's words. He understood that being a 'Bhovadi' was a trap that kept him separated from others, while being 'akiñcanaṃ' would allow him to be truly free. He eventually dropped his 'Bho' attitude and began to seek the wealth of 'having nothing,' eventually finding the peace that status could never provide.

word_meanings.term: Na cāhaṁ brāhmaṇaṁ brūmi yonijaṁ mattisambhavaṁ
word_meanings.meaning: I do not call one a brahmin simply because of being born from a (certain) womb,

word_meanings.term: bhovādī nāma so hoti sace hoti sakiñcano
word_meanings.meaning: that one is just one who says ‘bho’ if he is attached;
having nothing and unattached, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: I don’t call someone a brahmin after the mother’s womb they’re born from. If they still have attachments, they’re just someone who says “worthy”. Having nothing, taking nothing:...
verses.seo_description: I don’t call someone a brahmin after the mother’s womb they’re born from. If they still have attachments, they’re just someone who says “worthy”. Having...

verses.pali:
Sabbasaṁyojanaṁ chetvā, yo ve na paritassati;
Saṅgātigaṁ visaṁyuttaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Having cut off all fetters they have no anxiety. They’ve slipped their chains and are detached: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Sabbasaṃyojanaṃ' (all fetters) refers to the ten bonds that keep a being tied to the wheel of Samsara. 'Na paritassati' (does not tremble) means the absence of the 'shaking' caused by fear of loss or death.

story.title: The Story of the Fearless Monk
story.content: There was a monk who lived in a cemetery, a place that most people avoided because of the fear of ghosts and the reality of death. While others 'trembled' (paritassati) at the thought of spending even a single night there, this monk remained perfectly calm and focused. One night, a group of travelers who were lost asked him how he could live in such a terrifying place without fear. The monk explained that 'fear' is only possible for someone who still has 'Samyojanas'—fetters or bonds—to the world. He told them that when you 'cut off all fetters' (sabbasaṃyojanaṃ chetvā), there is nothing left for fear to grab onto. Most people fear death because they are 'attached' (sanga) to their bodies, their families, and their future. But the monk had 'overcome all attachments' and was 'visaṃyuttaṃ' (released). He explained that he wasn't 'brave' in the sense of fighting his fear; he simply had no reason to be afraid because he no longer viewed his 'self' as something that could be harmed. The Buddha later used this monk’s example to define the true 'Brahmana.' He said that the 'shaking' of the heart is a sign that there is still something we are 'grasping.' When the grasping stops, the shaking stops. The monk who has cut the fetters is like a man who has stepped out of a falling building; he is no longer worried because he is no longer inside the structure that is collapsing. The travelers were so moved by this explanation of 'fearlessness through non-attachment' that they felt a sense of peace even in the cemetery. They realized that the real ghosts are the 'fetters' of our own minds, and the only way to be safe is to 'cut them off' once and for all.

verses.transliteration:
Sabbasamyojanam chetva, yo ve na paritassati;
Sangatigam visamyuttam, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.term: Sabbasaṁyojanaṁ chetvā yo ve na paritassati
word_meanings.meaning: Whoever has cut off all the fetters surely does not tremble,
surmounting attachments, detached, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Having cut off all fetters they have no anxiety. They’ve slipped their chains and are detached: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Having cut off all fetters they have no anxiety. They’ve slipped their chains and are detached: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Chetvā naddhiṁ varattañca, sandānaṁ sahanukkamaṁ;
Ukkhittapalighaṁ buddhaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: They’ve cut the strap and harness, the halter and bridle too, with cross-bar lifted, they’re awakened: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: This verse uses the metaphor of a cart or a chariot. The 'Nandi' (strap), 'Varatta' (thong), and 'Sandāna' (rope) are the different parts that keep the animal harnessed to the cart of suffering. The 'Paḷigha' (bar) is the heavy bolt that keeps the door of liberation locked.

story.title: The Metaphor of the Chariot
story.content: A group of monks was watching a team of oxen pulling a heavy cart through the mud. They noticed how the oxen were bound by a series of 'straps,' 'thongs,' and 'ropes,' and how even if they tried to pull away, they were held in place by a heavy 'bar' across the gate of the pen. The Buddha, seeing an opportunity for a lesson, explained that human beings are exactly like those oxen. We are harnessed to the 'cart' of suffering (Dukkha) by different types of mental bonds. 'Nandi' (the strap) represents hatred, which pulls us into conflict. 'Varatta' (the thong) represents craving, which pulls us toward pleasant objects. 'Sandāna' (the rope) represents the false views and dogmas that keep our minds restricted. And the 'appurtenances' (sahanukkamaṃ) are the 'latent tendencies'—the habits from past lives that stay in the subconscious. The Buddha taught that a true 'Brahmana' is one who has 'cut' all these bindings. But even after the ropes are cut, one must still 'lift the bar' (ukkhittapaḷighaṃ)—the heavy bolt of ignorance (Avijja) that keeps the mind from seeing the Truth. Once the bar is lifted, the person is 'Buddha'—fully awakened and enlightened. He told the monks that as long as even one 'thong' remains, the oxen cannot be free. True liberation requires a total 'cutting' of the harness and a total 'lifting' of the bar. The monks realized that their own 'straps' and 'thongs' were the reasons they felt so burdened. They began to work on each 'binding' systematically, understanding that the 'cart' of Samsara only moves because we allow ourselves to be harnessed to it. By 'lifting the bar' of ignorance, they eventually found the freedom that lies beyond the 'pen' of conditioned existence.

verses.transliteration:
Chetva naddhim varattanca, sandanam sahanukkamam;
Ukkhittapaligham buddham, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: (Whoever) has cut off the thong, the strap, the rope, together with the bridle,
who has thrown off the obstacle and is awakened, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: They’ve cut the strap and harness, the halter and bridle too, with cross-bar lifted, they’re awakened: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: They’ve cut the strap and harness, the halter and bridle too, with cross-bar lifted, they’re awakened: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Akkosaṁ vadhabandhañca, aduṭṭho yo titikkhati;
Khantībalaṁ balānīkaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Abuse, killing, caging: they endure these without anger. Patience is their powerful army: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Khantībalaṃ' (the power of patience) is described as an 'army' (balānīkaṃ). This is a unique reversal of traditional military imagery, where the 'weapon' is actually a non-reactive, enduring heart.

story.title: The Army of Patience
story.content: A king who was very proud of his military strength once heard about a monk who was staying in his territory and who claimed that 'patience' was the greatest power in the world. The king, thinking this was a sign of weakness, decided to humiliate the monk. He sent his soldiers to 'reproach' (akkosaṃ) the monk with insults, to give him 'strokes' (vadha) with whips, and even to put him in 'bonds' (bandhaṃ). Throughout the entire ordeal, the monk remained 'aduṭṭho'—without a single thought of anger or revenge. He looked at the king and the soldiers with the same calm kindness he showed to his own disciples. When the king saw this, he was baffled. He asked the monk, 'Where is your army? How can you withstand my power without a single soldier?' The Buddha, who was visiting the region, explained the situation to the king. He said that this monk has an army that is far stronger than any king’s: 'khantībalaṃ balānīkaṃ'—the army of patience. While a physical army can be defeated by a larger one, the 'army of patience' cannot be touched because it does not fight back; it simply 'endures' (titikkhati) until the attacker's anger burns itself out. The Buddha taught that 'patience' is the ultimate 'power' because it is internal and unshakable. A person who can endure 'strokes and bonds' without hatred is a true 'Brahmana.' The king realized that his own 'power' was fragile because it depended on the fear of others, while the monk’s power was absolute because it depended on his own internal peace. He apologized to the monk and learned that the highest form of 'strength' is not the ability to inflict pain, but the ability to remain kind while enduring it. This story transformed the king’s approach to leadership, as he began to value 'Khanti' over his military might.

verses.transliteration:
Akkosam vadhabandhanca, aduttho yo titikkhati;
Khantibalam balanikam, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: Whoever, being pure, forbears with punishment, bondage and abuse,
having the strength of endurance, having an army of strengths,, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Abuse, killing, caging: they endure these without anger. Patience is their powerful army: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Abuse, killing, caging: they endure these without anger. Patience is their powerful army: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Akkodhanaṁ vatavantaṁ, sīlavantaṁ anussadaṁ;
Dantaṁ antimasārīraṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Not irritable or pretentious, dutiful in precepts and observances, tamed, bearing their final body: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Anussadaṃ' literally means 'without swelling,' referring to the absence of the 'swelling' of pride or craving. 'Antimasārīraṃ' means the physical body is the last one he will ever inhabit.

story.title: The Story of the Final Body
story.content: A group of monks were discussing what the 'end' of the path looked like. They wondered if an enlightened person still felt the physical sensations of the body or if they became like statues. The Buddha explained that a true 'Brahmana' still has a body, but it is his 'antimasārīraṃ'—his final body. This means that when this body dies, there will be no more 'rebirth' in any realm of existence. The Buddha described such a person as being 'akkodhanaṃ' (free from anger) and 'dantaṃ' (self-controlled). Even if they are physically sick or in pain, their minds do not 'swell' (anussadaṃ) with self-pity, complaint, or desire for health. They are 'sīlavantaṃ' (virtuous) not because they are following rules, but because their very nature has become pure. He compared them to a traveler who has reached his destination and is just waiting for the final sunset. There is no more 'craving' for a new journey or a new house. The 'religious duties' (vatavantaṃ) they observe are done out of habit and respect for the community, not because they are trying to 'get' something for themselves. The Buddha taught that 'bearing the final body' is a state of immense lightness. You are like a tenant who knows his lease is up and has already packed his bags; there is no anxiety, just a quiet readiness for the 'cessation' of the conditioned world. The monks realized that the goal was not to become a statue, but to become a person whose 'internal fire' had completely gone out, leaving only the cool, clear presence of a mind that is finished with the world.

verses.transliteration:
Akkodhanam vatavantam, silavantam anussadam;
Dantam antimasariram, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: (Whoever is) controlled of mind, dutiful, virtuous, taint-free,
well-trained and in his last body, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Not irritable or pretentious, dutiful in precepts and observances, tamed, bearing their final body: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Not irritable or pretentious, dutiful in precepts and observances, tamed, bearing their final body: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Vāri pokkharapatteva, āraggeriva sāsapo;
Yo na limpati kāmesu, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Like water from a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed off a pin-point, sensual pleasures slip off them: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: This verse provides two perfect visual metaphors for 'non-adherence.' The 'Mustard seed' (sāsapo) is too small and hard to 'stick' to a sharp point, just as the mind of a holy man is too refined for coarse pleasures to find a 'grip' on it.

story.title: The Lotus Leaf and the Mustard Seed
story.content: A young monk once asked the Buddha how it was possible to live in the world, surrounded by beautiful sights and delicious foods, without being corrupted by them. He felt that the world was like a sticky web that caught everyone who touched it. The Buddha gave him two vivid images to contemplate. First, he told him to look at the 'lotus leaf' (pokkharapatteva). Even though the lotus grows in the water, its leaf has a waxy surface so that 'water' (vāri) simply cannot soak into it; it just rolls off in perfect droplets. Second, he asked the monk to imagine trying to balance a 'mustard seed' (sāsapo) on the very 'point of a needle' (āragge). The seed is so small and hard that it cannot be held by the point; it slips off instantly. The Buddha taught that a true 'Brahmana' is like these two objects. He lives in the world of 'sensual pleasures' (kāmesu), but he 'does not cling' (na lippati) to them. His mind is 'waxy' with wisdom and 'sharp' with mindfulness, so that pleasures cannot find a 'grip' or a 'hold' on his heart. They come and go, but they never 'soak in.' He explained that the problem is not the 'water' (the world), but the 'surface' (the mind). If your mind is 'absorbent' like a sponge, you will be full of the world’s sorrows. But if your mind is like the lotus leaf, you can move through the deepest pond and remain perfectly dry. The young monk understood that his goal was not to 'destroy' the world, but to change the 'surface' of his own heart so that the world’s 'water' could no longer stick to him.

verses.transliteration:
Vari pokkharapatteva, araggeriva sasapo;
Yo na limpati kamesu, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: Like water on the lotus leaf, like a mustard seed on a needle,

word_meanings.term: yo na lippati kāmesu, tam-ahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ
word_meanings.meaning: he who is unsmeared by desires, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Like water from a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed off a pin-point, sensual pleasures slip off them: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Like water from a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed off a pin-point, sensual pleasures slip off them: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Yo dukkhassa pajānāti, idheva khayamattano;
Pannabhāraṁ visaṁyuttaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: They understand for themselves the end of suffering in this life; with burden put down, detached: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Pannabhāraṃ' (one who has laid down the burden) refers to the heavy load of the five aggregates (Khandhas) that we carry through life with the idea that they are 'me.'

story.title: The Story of the Laid-Down Burden
story.content: A man who had spent his whole life working hard to provide for his family and his village once sat down next to the Buddha, sighing with exhaustion. He said, 'Lord, I feel as though I am carrying a mountain on my back every day. When will this burden ever end?' The Buddha, with great compassion, explained that there are two types of burdens. There is the external burden of work and responsibility, and there is the internal burden of 'self.' He told the man that most of our 'suffering' (dukkhassa) comes from carrying the 'five aggregates' (body, feelings, etc.) as if they were a permanent, heavy weight that we must protect and feed. A true 'Brahmana' is one who 'realizes for himself' (pajānāti) that this burden is actually optional. Through meditation, he sees that the 'self' is just a bundle of changing processes. When he realizes this, he 'lays down the burden' (pannabhāraṃ). He doesn't wait for death to be free; he finds 'the end of suffering' (khayamattano) 'in this very life' (idheva). The Buddha compared him to a man who has been carrying a heavy sack of rocks, thinking it was gold; the moment he looks inside and sees only rocks, he drops the sack and feels an incredible sense of relief. He is then 'visaṃyuttaṃ'—released. The man realized that his physical exhaustion was manageable, but his 'mental weight' was what was truly crushing him. He began to learn how to 'drop the sack' of his ego, eventually finding the lightness of being that the Buddha described. He learned that the only way to 'rest' is not to stop working, but to stop carrying the 'mountain' of the self while you work.

verses.transliteration:
Yo dukkhassa pajanati, idheva khayamattano;
Pannabharam visamyuttam, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: Whoever knows right here the destruction of his suffering,
putting down the burden, detached, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: They understand for themselves the end of suffering in this life; with burden put down, detached: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: They understand for themselves the end of suffering in this life; with burden put down, detached: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Gambhīrapaññaṁ medhāviṁ, maggāmaggassa kovidaṁ;
Uttamatthamanuppattaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Deep in wisdom, intelligent, expert in the path and what is not the path; arrived at the highest goal: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Maggāmaggassa kovidaṃ' means being an expert (kovida) in knowing what leads to Nirvana (Magga) and what leads away from it (Amagga). This is the pinnacle of spiritual discernment.

story.title: The Skill of Discerning the Path
story.content: A monk was once lost in a dense, dark forest and was terrified by the many different trails that seemed to lead in every direction. He didn't know which one was safe and which one led to a cliff or a tiger’s den. When he finally found his way back to the Buddha, he asked how a person could navigate the even more confusing 'forest of life.' The Buddha told him that a true 'Brahmana' is one who has 'deep wisdom' (gambhīrapaññaṃ) and is 'medhāviṃ' (a man of knowledge). But his most important skill is being 'maggāmaggassa kovidaṃ'—an expert in discerning the 'right' path from the 'wrong' path. The Buddha explained that the 'wrong path' is anything that increases greed, hatred, and delusion, no matter how attractive or 'spiritual' it might look. The 'right path' is the one that leads to the 'highest goal' (uttamatthamanuppattaṃ)—the total ending of suffering. He compared the wise person to a seasoned mountain guide who can look at a single footprint or a broken twig and know exactly where a trail leads. Such a guide never gets lost because he understands the 'logic' of the mountain. In the same way, the wise monk understands the 'logic' of the mind. He knows that certain thoughts lead to pain and others lead to peace. Because he is 'skilled' in this discernment, he reaches the destination without wasting any time on detours. The monk realized that 'wisdom' is not about knowing everything, but about knowing the 'one' direction that leads to freedom. He stopped following every 'interesting' trail in his mind and stayed focused on the path of mindfulness, eventually becoming a guide for others.

verses.transliteration:
Gambhirapannam medhavim, maggamaggassa kovidam;
Uttamatthamanuppattam, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: The deeply wise sagacious one, skilled in what is path and not path,
who has reached the ultimate good, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Deep in wisdom, intelligent, expert in the path and what is not the path; arrived at the highest goal: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Deep in wisdom, intelligent, expert in the path and what is not the path; arrived at the highest goal: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Asaṁsaṭṭhaṁ gahaṭṭhehi, anāgārehi cūbhayaṁ;
Anokasārimappicchaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Mixing with neither householders nor the homeless; a migrant with no bastion, few in wishes: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Asaṃsaṭṭhaṃ' means 'unmixed' or 'not entangled.' This verse describes the ultimate 'internal' solitude. Even when around others, the monk remains emotionally and mentally independent.

story.title: The Solitary Wanderer
story.content: There was a monk named Sariputta who was often surrounded by large groups of people—both 'householders' (gahaṭṭhehi) seeking advice and 'houseless' monks (anāgārehi) seeking instruction. However, even in the middle of a crowd, Sariputta always seemed to be 'aloof' (asaṃsaṭṭhaṃ). This wasn't because he was cold or arrogant, but because his mind was 'anokasāri'—it did not 'dwell' or take 'abode' in any relationship or external situation. He was like the wind that passes through a village but doesn't live there. A group of monks asked the Buddha how Sariputta could be so busy and yet so solitary. The Buddha explained that a true 'Brahmana' is 'appicchaṃ'—free from desires or 'having few wants.' Because he doesn't want anything from people—no praise, no fame, no comfort—he doesn't get 'mixed up' in their lives. He is like a man who goes to a market with no money and no intention to buy anything; he sees everything, but he is not 'involved' in the trade. The Buddha taught that 'solitude' is not about where you are, but about what you want. If you are in a cave but you are dreaming of home, you are not solitary. But if you are in a city and you want nothing from it, you are 'wandering without a fixed abode.' This state of being 'unmixed' allows the monk to be truly helpful to others without being dragged down by their dramas. Sariputta’s example showed the monks that they could be social without being 'entangled,' provided their internal 'house' was empty of craving. They learned that the highest form of 'homelessness' is when the mind no longer looks for a place to hide in the hearts or minds of others.

verses.transliteration:
Asamsattham gahatthehi, anagarehi cubhayam;
Anokasarimappiccham, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: (Whoever) doesn’t mix with either householders or the houseless,
wandering homeless, with few desires, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Mixing with neither householders nor the homeless; a migrant with no bastion, few in wishes: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Mixing with neither householders nor the homeless; a migrant with no bastion, few in wishes: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Nidhāya daṇḍaṁ bhūtesu, tasesu thāvaresu ca;
Yo na hanti na ghāteti, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: They’ve laid aside violence against creatures firm and frail; not killing or making others kill: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: 'Tasesu' (the trembling) refers to those still subject to craving and fear, while 'Thāvaresu' (the firm) refers to the enlightened ones. 'Laying aside the stick' (nidhāya daṇḍaṃ) is a total commitment to non-injury.

story.title: The Laying Aside of the Stick
story.content: A former soldier who had seen much violence in his life once became a monk. Even though he had renounced war, he still found himself carrying a heavy walking stick and using it to hit stray dogs or to clear insects out of his path with unnecessary force. The Buddha, seeing that the 'habit of violence' was still in the man's heart, gave him this teaching. He said that a true 'Brahmana' is one who has 'laid aside the stick' (nidhāya daṇḍaṃ) toward 'all living beings.' He made a distinction between 'the trembling' (tasesu)—ordinary creatures who are full of fear and craving—and 'the firm' (thāvaresu)—beings who are already enlightened. A holy person does not differentiate; his compassions covers everyone. He 'neither kills nor causes others to kill' (na hanti na ghāteti). The Buddha explained that the 'stick' in our hand is just a reflection of the 'stick' in our mind—the desire to control or dominate others through fear. By 'laying aside the stick,' the monk is making a declaration that he is no longer a threat to any living thing. This total 'Ahimsa' (non-violence) creates a 'field of safety' around the monk. Animals and even people can feel when someone is truly harmless. The former soldier realized that his walking stick was a symbol of his old life. He put it away and practiced 'Metta' until his heart was so soft that even the smallest insect had nothing to fear from him. He learned that true power is not the ability to destroy life, but the ability to protect and respect it in all its forms, whether it is 'trembling' in fear or 'firm' in peace.

verses.transliteration:
Nidhaya dandam bhutesu, tasesu thavaresu ca;
Yo na hanti na ghateti, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.term: Nidhāya daṇḍaṁ bhūtesu tasesu thāvaresu ca
word_meanings.meaning: * Whoever has laid down the stick (used) against fearful and fearless beings,

word_meanings.term: yo na hanti na ghāteti, tam-ahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ
word_meanings.meaning: who neither hurts nor kills, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: They’ve laid aside violence against creatures firm and frail; not killing or making others kill: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: They’ve laid aside violence against creatures firm and frail; not killing or making others kill: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Aviruddhaṁ viruddhesu, attadaṇḍesu nibbutaṁ;
Sādānesu anādānaṁ, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: Not fighting among those who fight, quenched among those who have taken up arms, not grasping among those who grasp: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: This verse describes 'Upekkha' (equanimity). It is easy to be peaceful among friends, but a true Brahmin remains 'Nibbuta' (cool/peaceful) even when surrounded by 'Attadaṇḍesu' (those who have taken up sticks/violence).

story.title: The Story of the Peaceful Recluse
story.content: In a certain village, two rival clans were on the verge of a bloody war over the water rights of a local river. The air was thick with 'hostility' (viruddhesu), and men on both sides had 'taken up sticks' and swords (attadaṇḍesu), ready to strike. In the middle of this tense standoff sat a monk in deep meditation. Despite the shouting and the clashing of weapons around him, he remained 'aviruddhaṃ'—completely friendly and non-antagonistic. He did not take sides, nor did he show fear. One of the clan leaders, frustrated by the monk's stillness, demanded to know why he wasn't helping his kin. The Buddha, who arrived to mediate the dispute, used the monk as a living example of a true Brahmin. He explained that most people are like dry wood; one spark of hostility makes them burst into flames. But the enlightened person is like water. Even when surrounded by the 'violent,' he remains 'peaceful.' Among those who are 'attached' (sādānesu) to their land and their pride, the Brahmin remains 'unattached' (anādānaṃ). The Buddha taught that the monk's silence was more powerful than the clans' shouting, as it came from a mind that had already 'won' the only war that matters—the war against internal anger. Seeing the monk's profound 'coolness' (nibbutaṃ), the warriors felt ashamed of their own heat. They lowered their weapons and realized that the river's water was not worth the blood of their neighbors. The monk’s equanimity served as a mirror, showing the clans that peace is a choice one makes internally, regardless of external provocation.

verses.transliteration:
Aviruddham viruddhesu, attadandesu nibbutam;
Sadanesu anadanam, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: Being friendly with the hostile, calm amongst those holding a stick,
not attached amongst those attached, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: Not fighting among those who fight, quenched among those who have taken up arms, not grasping among those who grasp: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: Not fighting among those who fight, quenched among those who have taken up arms, not grasping among those who grasp: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Yassa rāgo ca doso ca, māno makkho ca pātito;
Sāsaporiva āraggā, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: They’ve discarded greed and hate, along with conceit and contempt, like a mustard seed off the point of a pin: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: This reinforces the mustard seed metaphor (see verse 401). Here, the Buddha lists four specific 'heavy' defilements that simply cannot find a foothold on the 'sharp' mind of an Arhat.

story.title: The Falling of the Defilements
story.content: A young novice once asked a senior Arhat monk if he ever had to fight against 'pride' (māno) or 'hypocrisy' (makkho). The novice found that even when he was doing good deeds, a small voice in his head wanted praise, and another part of him wanted to hide his mistakes. The senior monk smiled and used a simple visual aid. He took a needle and tried to place a small 'mustard seed' (sāsapo) on its tip. Every time he let go, the seed immediately 'fell off' (pātito). He explained that in the beginning of the path, the mind is like a wide, sticky table; every piece of 'dust' like lust or hatred sticks to it and stays there. But through the constant practice of mindfulness, the mind becomes 'sharp' and refined, like the 'point of a needle' (āragga). When the mind reaches this state of total clarity, defilements like pride and hypocrisy simply have no 'surface area' to cling to. They might arise as momentary shadows, but they cannot take root. The Buddha, confirming this teaching, said that a true Brahmin is one whose internal 'surface' is so refined that the 'grease' of the world cannot stain it. He taught that we shouldn't spend our lives fighting each individual thought, but rather focus on 'sharpening' our wisdom. Once the wisdom is sharp enough, the 'falling off' of lust and hatred becomes a natural and effortless process. The novice realized that his struggle was due to the 'stickiness' of his own ego, and he began to work on refining his awareness until his mind, too, became a needle-point upon which no worldly seed could rest.

verses.transliteration:
Yassa rago ca doso ca, mano makkho ca patito;
Sasaporiva aragga, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.term: Yassa rāgo ca doso ca māno makkho ca pātito
word_meanings.meaning: Whoever has dropped off passion and hatred, conceit and anger,
like a mustard seed from a needle, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: They’ve discarded greed and hate, along with conceit and contempt, like a mustard seed off the point of a pin: that’s who I declare a brahmin.

verses.pali:
Akakkasaṁ viññāpaniṁ, giraṁ saccamudīraye;
Yāya nābhisaje kañci, tamahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ.
verses.translation: The words they utter are polished, informative, and true, and don’t offend anyone: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.commentary: A Brahmin's speech must meet three criteria: it must be 'Akakkasaṃ' (gentle/not harsh), 'Viññāpaniṃ' (instructive/meaningful), and 'Saccaṃ' (true). Even if a truth is unpleasant, the Brahmin finds a way to speak it without 'offending' (nābhisaje).

story.title: The Art of Right Speech
story.content: There was a monk named Pilindavaccha who had a very strange habit. Although he was an Arhat and deeply compassionate, he had a lingering linguistic habit from his past lives as a high-caste Brahmin of addressing everyone—even laypeople—as 'outcastes.' He didn't mean it as an insult, but the word itself was 'harsh' (kakkasa) to the ears of others. People complained to the Buddha, saying that a holy man shouldn't use such offensive language. The Buddha called Pilindavaccha and, through his divine eye, saw that the monk had been born into the Brahmin caste for five hundred consecutive lifetimes. The habit was purely 'vasana' (residual tendency) and contained no 'intent to offend' (nābhisaje). However, the Buddha used this as an opportunity to teach the 'Gold Standard' of speech for a Brahmin. He said that a holy person should strive for speech that is 'Akakkasaṃ'—gentle like silk—and 'Viññāpaniṃ'—full of instruction that helps others understand the Dhamma. Most importantly, it must be 'Saccaṃ' (true). The Buddha explained that true speech is like a surgeon's knife; it might cut, but it is done with a gentle hand and only for the purpose of healing. He taught that even if one has no 'evil intent,' one should be mindful of how their 'giraṃ' (words) land in the hearts of others. Pilindavaccha, realizing the discomfort he was causing, used his mindfulness to override his ancient habits. The monks learned that the highest speech is not just about 'not lying,' but about the active creation of harmony through every word uttered.

verses.transliteration:
Akakkasam vinnapanim, giram saccamudiraye;
Yaya nabhisaje kanci, tamaham brumi brahmanam.

word_meanings.meaning: (Whoever) speaks a word of truth that is informed and is not coarse,

word_meanings.term: yāya nābhisaje kañci, tam-ahaṁ brūmi brāhmaṇaṁ
word_meanings.meaning: through which no one would be angry, that one I say is a brahmin.

verses.share_excerpt: The words they utter are polished, informative, and true, and don’t offend anyone: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
verses.seo_description: The words they utter are polished, informative, and true, and don’t offend anyone: that’s who I declare a brahmin.
